Kinematic cosmic dipole from a large sample of strong lenses

Martin Millon    Charles Dalang    Thomas Collett    Camille Bonvin
Abstract

Measurements of the kinematic cosmic dipole continue to show an intriguing tension between the value inferred from the CMB and that obtained from high-redshift source number counts. While the measured dipole direction appears consistent, the amplitude, set by the observer’s peculiar velocity vov_{o}, remains in significant disagreement. In this paper, we propose using strong gravitational lenses with well-measured Einstein radii to estimate the kinematic cosmic dipole, through the relativistic distortion of the Einstein angle induced by the observer’s motion. We show that this effect could be detected solely from measurements of the Einstein radius in wide, high-resolution imaging surveys such as Euclid. However, the precision achievable using Einstein-radius measurements alone, without redshift or lens-galaxy mass information, appears insufficient to discriminate between the CMB value of vov_{o} and that derived from source number counts at high statistical significance. Nevertheless, we demonstrate that including a large sample of lenses with available kinematic information, either via the Fundamental Plane relation or, ideally, through spectroscopic velocity-dispersion measurements, drastically reduces the noise and substantially improves the constraining power of this method. We show that, for a realistic sample of strong lenses detected by Euclid and complemented with spectroscopic velocity dispersion measurements from 4MOST or DESI, it is possible to discriminate between the CMB- and source-number-counts–inferred values at the ∼4​σ\sim 4\sigma level using a new, fully independent method. We further demonstrate that this technique is only weakly sensitive to strong-lensing selection effects, with selection biases and threshold effects estimated to be well below the 1% level.

1 Introduction

Using measurements of the dipole anisotropy in the blackbody temperature of the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB), the Planck satellite has accurately determined both the magnitude and direction of the observer’s velocity. The observer’s motion produces a temperature modulation of order δ​T/⟨T⟩∼𝒪​(10−3)\delta T/\langle T\rangle\sim\mathcal{O}(10^{-3}) around the angularly averaged temperature ⟨T⟩\langle T\rangle. This effect allows for a precise measurement of the observer’s velocity, with magnitude vo=369.82±0.11​km​s−1,v_{o}=369.82\pm 0.11\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}, directed towards 𝒗^o=(264.021∘±0.011∘,48.253∘±0.005∘)\hat{\bm{v}}_{o}=(264.021^{\circ}\pm 0.011^{\circ},48.253^{\circ}\pm 0.005^{\circ}) in Galactic coordinates [Fixsen:1994, Fixsen:1996, Aghanim:2018eyx, Planck:2013kqc]. This interpretation is valid under the assumption that the intrinsic CMB dipole due to primordial density perturbations, expected to be of order 𝒪​(10−5−10−6)\mathcal{O}(10^{-5}-10^{-6}), is small in comparison.

The observer’s velocity also introduces two other signatures in the CMB: a Doppler modulation effect, which amplifies the apparent temperature fluctuations in the velocity direction and reduces them in the opposite direction, and an aberration effect, which changes the angular scales of the temperature fluctuations. These two effects induce correlations between the ll and l±1l\pm 1 multipoles of the CMB. Both of these effects have been detected [Planck:2013kqc, Ferreira:2020aqa, Saha:2021bay], leading to results consistent with the velocity inferred from the dipole, and thus supporting the entirely kinematic interpretation of the CMB dipole. However, the relatively large error bars still allow for an intrinsic component at the level of ∼40%\sim 40\% of the CMB dipole [Schwarz:2016].

On the other hand, the observer’s velocity can be measured independently using high-redshift source number counts, such as quasars, following a method originally proposed by [Ellis1984]. The observer velocity generates a dipolar modulation in the source number counts, due both to aberration and to threshold effects, i.e. the fact that sources will appear brighter in the velocity direction, increasing the number of sources above the flux threshold. In such analyzes, it is crucial to consider sources distributed over a sufficiently large cosmological volume in order to minimize contamination from local structures. Recent measurements based on quasar number counts from large imaging surveys point toward a discrepant value of the observer’s velocity, with several analyzes reporting a tension at the level of 33–5.7​σ5.7\sigma [Secrest:2020has, Dam:2022wwh, Secrest:2022uvx]. However, contamination by low-redshift sources affected by local clustering [Oayda2024] could account for part of this discrepancy. Evolution of the source population was proposed as a possible explanation for the tension [Dalang:2021ruy], until it was shown not to affect the number count dipole, if source properties are measured close to the flux limit [vonHausegger:2024jan]. Overall, while measurements based on the Quaia catalog [Storey-Fisher2024] seem not to support the presence of such a tension [Mittal2024, Abghari2024], a recent re-analysis of the CatWISE data still finds discrepant results at the ∼3​σ\sim 3\sigma level, even after accounting for possible local clustering effects [Bashir2025]. Redshift tomography [vonHausegger:2024fcu] and measurements of ll and l+1l+1 correlations in source number counts [Lacasa:2024ybp] were proposed as powerful complementary methods, allowing to cleanly disentangle threshold effects from the observer velocity. A review of the current status of these observations, potential systematics impacting these measurements, and cosmological implication of the CMB dipole anomaly can be found in [secrest2025, Secrest2025c]. This lack of consensus highlights the need for independent methods to measure the observer’s velocity.

In this work, we propose an independent measurement of the observer’s peculiar velocity using strong gravitational lenses. Our method relies solely on angular measurements on the sky, providing a very robust way of extracting the observer velocity. The observable is derived purely from special relativity: a non-comoving observer perceives relativistic aberration, which induces a direction-dependent modulation of the observed Einstein radius. We demonstrate that selection effects, that modulate the number of detected Einstein rings across the sky, have a completely negligible impact on the measured dipole, below 0.1%. As such, this observable is insensitive to flux-related and clustering systematics that may bias source-count dipole experiments. This measurement, however, requires a very large number of strong gravitational lenses to detect a small distortion of the Einstein ring for lenses aligned with the observer velocity.

Euclid is expected to find about 𝒪​(105)\mathcal{O}(10^{5}) strong gravitational lenses by the end of the mission, a 2 order of magnitude increase compared to the 𝒪​(103)\mathcal{O}(10^{3}) lenses known from Stage-III imaging surveys. Already in the first Q1 data release, covering only 63 square-degrees, Euclid has revealed 497 high-quality new lens candidates [EuclidWalmsley2025] thanks to the combination of computer vision methods and human visual inspection [Euclid2025Rojas, EuclidLines2025, EuclidHolloway2025]. According to [EuclidWalmsley2025], this is expected to scale to approximately 7,000 new strong lenses in the first Euclid data release (DR1) and to more than 10510^{5} lenses over the entire Euclid Wide survey. In addition, the image quality of Euclid enables very precise measurements of the Einstein angle, at the level of ∼1%\sim 1\%. As we demonstrate in this work, the observer’s peculiar velocity can be extracted using this observable alone. If spectroscopic redshifts for the background sources and the lens galaxy, as well as velocity dispersion information for the lens are available, even for a sub-set of all systems, the precision of the measurement can be significantly improved.

This paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we derive the equations describing the aberration of the Einstein angle induced by the observer’s velocity in strongly lensed systems. In Section 3, we introduce the Bayesian framework used to infer the observer’s velocity, present the simulated lens sample used to validate the method, and report the resulting constraints on the dipole amplitude and direction obtained using lensing information alone. In Section 4, we show how additional lens kinematic information, either from spectroscopic measurements or inferred via the Fundamental Plane (FP) relation, can be incorporated into the analysis to improve the constraints. Finally, we conclude and discuss the limitations of the method in Section 5. Throughout the paper, we assume the flat Λ\Lambda-Cold Dark Matter model with H0=70H_{0}=70 km​s−1​Mpc−1{\,\mathrm{km}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}\,\mathrm{Mpc}^{-1}} and Ωm=0.3\Omega_{m}=0.3 to convert redshifts into angular diameter distances.

2 Einstein angle aberration

The peculiar velocity of the observer, 𝒗o\bm{v}_{o}, leads to a distortion of the Einstein ring due to aberration, as described in [Sereno2008, Dalang2023]. Consequently, a perfectly circular Einstein ring of radius θE\theta_{E} will appear to a boosted observer as an ellipse, as shown in Fig. 1. To compute the distortion, without loss of generality, we align the 𝒆z\bm{e}_{z} axis along the observer velocity and we place the direction of the center of mass of the lens 𝒏^\bm{\hat{n}} in the 𝒆z−𝒆y\bm{e}_{z}-\bm{e}_{y} plane, as illustrated in Figure 1. A boosted observer will see the center of mass in the direction 𝒏^′≠𝒏^\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime}\neq\bm{\hat{n}}, which however still lies in the 𝒆z−𝒆y\bm{e}_{z}-\bm{e}_{y} plane. The Einstein ring seen by the boosted observer lives in the plane orthogonal to 𝒏^′\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime}. To describe its shape, we adopt the same coordinate system as in [Dalang2023], i.e. we define 𝒆^1′\bm{\hat{e}}_{1}^{\prime} along the 𝒆x\bm{e}_{x} direction, such that angles along that axis are preserved under the boost, and 𝒆^2′\bm{\hat{e}}_{2}^{\prime} lies in the 𝒆z−𝒆y\bm{e}_{z}-\bm{e}_{y} plane, orthogonally to 𝒏^′\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime}. Angles along 𝒆^2′\bm{\hat{e}}_{2}^{\prime} are deformed by the boost. We denote by θcm\theta_{\rm cm} the angle between 𝒗o\bm{v}_{o} and the unboosted center of mass of the lens, and by θmin\theta_{\rm min} and θmax\theta_{\rm max} the angle between 𝒗o\bm{v}_{o} and the unboosted extremities of the ring. The impact of the boost on an angle θ\theta, at linear order in vo/cv_{o}/c, is given by

θ′=θ−voc​sin⁡θ′.\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}=\theta-\frac{v_{o}}{c}\sin\theta^{\prime}\,. (2.1)

With this, we find that the boosted Einstein radius in direction 𝒆^2′\bm{\hat{e}}_{2}^{\prime} is given by

θE​2′=θmax′−θcm′=θE​2​(1−voc​cos⁡θcm′)=θcm′−θmin′.\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E2}=\theta^{\prime}_{\rm max}-\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}=\theta_{E2}\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)=\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}-\theta^{\prime}_{\rm min}\,. (2.2)

The fact that the boosted ring remains centered on the boosted center of mass is valid in the limit θE​2≪θcm\theta_{E2}\ll\theta_{\rm cm}. In contrast, in the 𝒆^1′\bm{\hat{e}}_{1}^{\prime} direction the boosted Einstein radius is the same as the unboosted one

θE​1′=θE​1.\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E1}=\theta_{E1}\,. (2.3)

The Einstein angle in an arbitrary direction can be expressed as a function of the azimuthal angle δ′\delta^{\prime}, defined relative to 𝒆^1′\bm{\hat{e}}^{\prime}_{1} :

θE′​(δ′)\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\delta^{\prime}) =θE​1′​(δ′)2+θE​2′​(δ′)2\displaystyle=\sqrt{\theta^{\prime}_{E1}(\delta^{\prime})^{2}+\theta^{\prime}_{E2}(\delta^{\prime})^{2}}
=θE​1​(δ′)2+θE​2​(δ′)2​(1−voc​cos⁡θcm′)2.\displaystyle=\sqrt{\theta_{E1}(\delta^{\prime})^{2}+\theta_{E2}(\delta^{\prime})^{2}\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)^{2}}. (2.4)

For simplicity, we assume that, for an observer at rest, the Einstein ring is a perfect circle, arising from a spherical mass distribution, such as a Singular Isothermal Sphere (SIS)

θE​1​(δ′)\displaystyle\theta_{E1}(\delta^{\prime}) =θE​cos⁡δ′,\displaystyle=\theta_{E}\cos\delta^{\prime}\,, (2.5)
θE​2​(δ′)\displaystyle\theta_{E2}(\delta^{\prime}) =θE​sin⁡δ′.\displaystyle=\theta_{E}\sin\delta^{\prime}\,. (2.6)

Inserting (2.5) and (2.6) into Eq. (2), we can relate the observed Einstein angle θE′​(δ′)\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\delta^{\prime}) seen by a moving observer to the comoving one θE\theta_{E}

θE′​(δ′)\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\delta^{\prime}) =θE​(1−sin2⁡δ′​voc​cos⁡θcm′).\displaystyle=\theta_{E}\left(1-\sin^{2}\delta^{\prime}\,\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)\,. (2.7)

In practice, the Einstein angle is measured by fitting a mass model, e.g. a SIS model, to the imaging data. Consequently, the measured Einstein angle is azimuthally average over δ′\delta^{\prime}:

θE′\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E} =12​π​∫02​πdδ′​θE​(1−sin2⁡δ′​voc​cos⁡θcm′)\displaystyle=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{2\pi}\mathrm{d}\delta^{\prime}\;\theta_{E}\left(1-\sin^{2}\delta^{\prime}\,\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)
=θE​(1−vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′).\displaystyle=\theta_{E}\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)\,. (2.8)

In the rest of this work, we use the average Einstein angle θE′\theta^{\prime}_{E} as our primary observable to measure 𝒗o\bm{v}_{o}.

To increase the statistics, we measure the mean size of all detected rings in a given solid angle d​Ω′\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}. Since the Einstein angle depends on the redshifts of the sources, zs′z^{\prime}_{s}, and of the lens, zl′z^{\prime}_{l}, this introduces two integrals weighted by the number of systems observed in that solid angle d​N​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}}

⟨θE′​(𝒏^′)⟩=(d​N​(𝒏^′)d​Ω′)−1​∫dzs′​∫dzl′​d​N​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′​θE′​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′),\displaystyle\langle\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})\rangle=\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}}\right)^{-1}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}}\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})\,, (2.9)

with

d​N​(𝒏^′)d​Ω′=∫dzs′​∫dzl′​d​N​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′.\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}}=\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}}\,. (2.10)

To evaluate Eq. (2.9), we need to express the Einstein radius as a function of the boosted redshifts zs′z^{\prime}_{s} and zl′z^{\prime}_{l}, which introduces additional distortions. The Einstein radius depends indeed on the distances to the source and to the lens, and fixed values of zs′z^{\prime}_{s} and zl′z^{\prime}_{l} correspond to different distances in different directions. As shown in Appendix A, the Einstein radius in redshift space is given by

θE′​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)=\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})= θE​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))​(1−vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′)+((1+zs)​∂θE∂zs+(1+zl)​∂θE∂zl)​voc​cos⁡θcm′,\displaystyle\theta_{E}(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)+\left((1+z_{s})\frac{\partial\theta_{E}}{\partial z_{s}}+(1+z_{l})\frac{\partial\theta_{E}}{\partial z_{l}}\right)\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,, (2.11)

where θE​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))\theta_{E}(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l})) is the unboosted Einstein ring evaluated at the comoving distances r​(zs′)r(z^{\prime}_{s}) and r​(zl′)r(z^{\prime}_{l}), which do not depend on direction.111Note that in the second term, we can use interchangeably zz or z′z^{\prime} since they multiply a term which is already linear in vo/cv_{o}/c From Eq. (2.11) we see that if we would measure the Einstein ring in bins of observed redshifts, the dipolar contribution would be affected by two terms: aberration and distance perturbations. However, once we integrate over all redshifts, the second contribution cancels and only aberration persists. To see this we need to express the number of detected Einstein rings in terms of zs′z_{s}^{\prime} and zl′z^{\prime}_{l}. The detailed calculation can be found in Appendix A. We obtain

d​N​(zs′,zl′,𝒏^′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm{d}N(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}} =d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​(1+4​voc​cos⁡θcm′)\displaystyle=\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\left(1+\frac{4v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right) (2.12)
+[(1+zs)​∂∂zs​(d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl)+(1+zl)​∂∂zl​(d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl)]​voc​cos⁡θcm′.\displaystyle+\left[(1+z_{s})\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{s}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\right)+(1+z_{l})\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{l}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\right)\right]\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,.

The second line in Eq. (2.12) combines with the 2​vo/c2v_{o}/c in the first line, and with the second term in Eq. (2.11) to give rise to a boundary term, which exactly vanishes (see Appendix A for more detail). Moreover the effect of aberration in the number of detected systems (the remaining 2​vo/c2v_{o}/c in the first line of Eq. (2.12)) cancels in the numerator and the denominator of Eq. (2.9). Due to aberration, we see more Einstein rings per solid angle in the direction of the boost, but since we normalise the Einstein radius by the number of detected Einstein rings, this effect does not impact the final result. With this, we obtain

⟨θE′​(𝒏^′)⟩=(1−vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′)​⟨θE⟩,\displaystyle\langle\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})\rangle=\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)\langle\theta_{E}\rangle\,, (2.13)

where ⟨θE⟩\langle\theta_{E}\rangle is the mean Einstein radius of the unboosted sample

⟨θE⟩=∫dzs′​∫dzl′​f​(zs′,zl′)​θE​(zs′,zl′),\displaystyle\langle\theta_{E}\rangle=\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}f(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})\theta_{E}(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})\,, (2.14)

with f​(zs′,zl′)f(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l}) the redshift distribution of systems

f​(zs′,zl′)=d​N​(zs′,zl′)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl∫dzs′​∫dzl′​d​N​(zs′,zl′)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl.\displaystyle f(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{d}N(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}}{\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}}\,. (2.15)

Eq. (2.13) allows us to measure the observer velocity vov_{o} by fitting a monopole term, proportional to the mean of the lens sample ⟨θE⟩\langle\theta_{E}\rangle and a dipole term proportional to vov_{o}. This relies on the underlying assumptions that there are no selection effects, i.e. that all systems are detected. In Section 5, we will relax this assumption, introducing a selection in flux and in size. We will show that this introduces additional contributions to the dipole, which are however much smaller than the effect of aberration. More precisely the selection in size introduces a contamination of the order of 0.1% while the selection in flux is three orders of magnitude smaller. This shows that Einstein rings provide a very robust observable to measure the observer velocity, without being contaminated by selection effects that are typically difficult to characterize.

Note that Eq. (2.13) has been derived in redshift space, since the source and lens redshifts can be measured. However, since we integrate over all systems, we could have instead chosen to integrate over comoving distances rsr_{s} and rlr_{l}, without changing the result. In this case, the Einstein radius is simply given by Eq. (2.8), and the number of systems is only affected by aberration d​Ω/d​Ω′=1+2​voc​cos⁡θcm′\mathrm{d}\Omega/\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}=1+2\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}. The final result is then the same as Eq. (2.13), with redshifts replaced by comoving distances.

3 Measurement of the observer velocity from lensing alone

3.1 Likelihood definition

We consider a catalog of NN azimuthally averaged Einstein radii measurements θEn′\theta^{\prime}_{E_{n}} with associated galactic coordinates (ln,bn)(l_{n},b_{n}), forming an angle θcm,n′\theta^{\prime}_{{\rm cm}_{,n}} with respect to the dipole direction (lol_{o}, bob_{o}). We define ⟨θE⟩\langle\theta_{E}\rangle as the mean Einstein radius of the sample, σθE\sigma_{\theta_{E}} as the standard deviation of the distribution, and δ​θEn′\delta\theta^{\prime}_{E_{n}} as the associated measurement error for each lens. One can fit a monopole and a dipole by maximizing the likelihood:

ln⁡ℒ1=−12​∑n=1N[θEn′−⟨θE⟩​(1−vo2​c​cos⁡(θcm,n′))]2δ​θEn2′+σθE2−12​∑n=1Nln⁡[2​π​(δ​θEn2′+σθE2)],\ln\mathcal{L}_{1}=-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\frac{\left[\theta^{\prime}_{E_{n}}-\langle\theta_{E}\rangle\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos(\theta^{\prime}_{{\rm cm}_{,n}})\right)\right]^{2}}{\delta\theta^{{}^{\prime}2}_{E_{n}}+\sigma_{\theta_{E}}^{2}}-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\ln\!\big[2\pi\big(\delta\theta^{{}^{\prime}2}_{E_{n}}+\sigma_{\theta_{E}}^{2}\big)\big]\,, (3.1)

where ⟨θE⟩\langle\theta_{E}\rangle, σθE\sigma_{\theta_{E}}, lol_{o}, bob_{o}, and vov_{o} are free parameters.

Here, we fit a displacement of the mean of the Einstein–radius distribution as a function of position on the sky. This probe has the advantage that it does not require any spectroscopic information about the lenses, not even the lens or source redshifts. The measurement relies solely on angle measurements in the plane of the sky. However, the intrinsic scatter of the distribution, σθE\sigma_{\theta_{E}}, constitutes a significant source of noise, driven primarily by the broad distribution of lens galaxy masses and the wide range of lens and source redshifts. The noise introduced by the unknown lens mass is analogous to the unknown intrinsic shape of the galaxy, introducing shape noise in weak lensing analysis. However, in contrast to weak lensing, the mass of the lensing galaxy can be estimated independently from other techniques. As we demonstrate in Section 4, incorporating spectroscopic redshift information for both the lens and the source, together with independent mass estimates (e.g., from velocity–dispersion measurements or from the FP relation; [Dressler1987, Djorgovski1987]), can substantially reduce this noise.

3.2 Lens population model

We use the LensPop [LensPop, Collett2015] lens simulation package to generate a realistic population of strong lenses expected from Euclid. LensPop draws deflector galaxies from the observed distributions of velocity dispersions and ellipticities measured in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey [Park2007]. Deflectors are assumed to be isothermal and are randomly distributed in comoving volume. Sources are placed behind the deflectors, with source redshifts and luminosities drawn from the catalog of [Connolly2010], which uses semi-analytic models to populate galaxies onto the dark matter halos of the Millennium Simulation [DeLucia2006].

This procedure results in a population of lenses representative of those present in the Universe; however, for our purposes, we require the subset of lenses that would be discovered by Euclid. To this end, LensPop simulates observations of this population with Euclid-like data quality and applies a simplified discovery selection function. As described in [Collett2015], this selection function can be summarized by requirements on the signal-to-noise ratio of the source, a minimum Einstein radius, a total source magnification greater than 3, and an arc length sufficient for the system to be resolved as an extended object.

The properties of the resulting lens sample are shown in Figure 2. While the simplifying assumptions of LensPop cannot perfectly reproduce the true Euclid selection function, they have been shown to accurately predict both the number of galaxy-galaxy lenses discoverable by Euclid and their Einstein radius distribution [AcevedoBarroso2025, EuclidWalmsley2025]. These are precisely the quantities that must be modeled reliably for our cosmic dipole inference forecast to be robust.

From this sample, we randomly assign galactic coordinates (ln,bn)(l_{n},b_{n}) to each lens in the catalog within a footprint that mimics the Euclid Wide Survey footprint, requiring |bn|>30|b_{n}|>30°. We simulate the observed θEn′\theta^{\prime}_{E_{n}} from the true θEn\theta_{E_{n}} using Eq. (2.8) and adding Gaussian noise with standard deviation δ​θEn′/θEn′\delta\theta^{\prime}_{E_{n}}/\theta^{\prime}_{E_{n}} = 0.02. Euclid could measure the Einstein radius with a precision of about 1%, however, we adopt a relative 2% uncertainty to account for additional error contributions from line-of-sight large-scale structures.

3.3 Lensing only results

From the simulated lens catalog described in Section 3.2, we randomly select samples of 100 000100\,000 (the “100k” sample) and 400 000400\,000 lenses (“400k” sample), from which we evaluate the lensing-only likelihood (Eq. 3.1). We then infer the posterior probability distributions of the parameters vov_{o}, lol_{o}, and bob_{o} using the Nautilus sampler [Nautilus]. The 1​σ1\sigma and 2​σ2\sigma two-dimensional posterior probability contours for these parameters are shown in Fig. 3. The remaining free parameters, ⟨θE⟩\langle\theta_{E}\rangle and σθE\sigma_{\theta_{E}}, are marginalized over and are not shown in this figure. Table 1 summarizes the marginalized posterior constraints obtained both with and without fixing the dipole direction. We repeat the analysis for two different true values of the observer’s peculiar velocity: vo,true=369.82v_{o,\rm true}=369.82km​s−1{\,\mathrm{km}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}}, corresponding to the CMB-inferred value, and vo,true=1109.46v_{o,\rm true}=1109.46 km​s−1{\,\mathrm{km}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}}, corresponding to three times the CMB value and consistent with recent results from source number counts [e.g. Secrest:2022uvx].

While the dipole is detected in both the “100k” and “400k” samples for the highest peculiar velocity, vo,true=1109.46v_{o,\rm true}=1109.46km​s−1{\,\mathrm{km}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}}, the lowest CMB-inferred value does not produce a sufficiently strong signal to be detected in either sample. Fixing the dipole direction leads to a moderate tightening of the constraints on the dipole amplitude, improving the 68% credible interval width on vov_{o} by approximately 13–16%, depending on the sample size and fiducial velocity.

We therefore find that, when relying on lensing information alone, the Euclid lens sample is unlikely to provide strong discriminatory power between the CMB-inferred dipole amplitude and the larger values suggested by source number count analyzes. The resulting constraints are not sufficiently precise to exclude the CMB-inferred value beyond the ∼1.1​σ\sim 1.1\sigma level, even when fixing the dipole direction and adopting the most optimistic 400 000400\,000-lens scenario. We explore the possibility of including the stellar kinematic information to improve the precision of this measurement in the next section.

4 Combining lensing and Stellar Kinematics

The dominant source of uncertainty in the measurement of the observer velocity arises from the width of the Einstein radius distribution, σθE\sigma_{\theta_{E}}. This uncertainty can be reduced by incorporating additional information that constrains the unboosted Einstein radius of each ring. We explore two sets of complementary datasets here.

4.1 Spectroscopic velocity dispersion

First, the stellar velocity dispersion of the lens in combination with the source and lens redshifts, provides information about its mass, which directly informs the Einstein radius. More precisely, for a SIS, the central stellar velocity dispersion of the lens galaxy σv\sigma_{v}, relates to the Einstein radius through:

σv=c​θE4​πdsdl​s,\sigma_{v}=c\,\sqrt{\frac{\theta_{E}}{4\pi}\,\frac{d_{s}}{d_{ls}}\,,} (4.1)

where dsd_{s} and dl​sd_{ls} are the angular diameter distances between the observer and the source and between the lens and the source, respectively. The dispersion σv\sigma_{v} is measurable from long-slit or fiber spectroscopic observations of the lens galaxy. It is unaffected by the observer’s peculiar velocity because it is a relative measurement of wavelength dispersion. This additional observable provides an estimate of the mass of each lensing galaxy, hence largely reducing the noise term in Eq. (3.1). The fractional measurement error on σv\sigma_{v} translates to θE\theta_{E} according to:

δ​σvσv=12​δ​θEθE.\frac{\delta\sigma_{v}}{\sigma_{v}}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{\delta\theta_{E}}{\theta_{E}}\,. (4.2)

However, the angular diameter distances dl​sd_{ls} and dsd_{s} in Eq. (4.1) have to be inferred from the observed redshifts zl′z_{l}^{\prime}, zs′z_{s}^{\prime}, and a cosmology, which are biased by the peculiar velocities of the observer, of the lens, and of the source. Using the same notation as in [Dalang2023], the observed redshifts zl′z_{l}^{\prime}, zs′z_{s}^{\prime} relate to the cosmological (or background) redshift zlz_{l}, zsz_{s} in the following way

(1+zl)\displaystyle(1+z_{l}) =(1+zl′)​(1+ZL​voc),\displaystyle=(1+z_{l}^{\prime})\left(1+Z_{L}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\,, (4.3)
(1+zs)\displaystyle(1+z_{s}) =(1+zs′)​(1+ZS​voc),\displaystyle=(1+z_{s}^{\prime})\left(1+Z_{S}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\,, (4.4)

with

ZL\displaystyle Z_{L} =𝒏^′⋅(𝒗o−𝒗l)vo,\displaystyle=\frac{\bm{\hat{n}^{\prime}}\cdot(\bm{v}_{o}-\bm{v}_{l})}{v_{o}}\,, (4.5)
ZS\displaystyle Z_{S} =𝒏^′⋅(𝒗o−𝒗s)vo.\displaystyle=\frac{\bm{\hat{n}^{\prime}}\cdot(\bm{v}_{o}-\bm{v}_{s})}{v_{o}}\,. (4.6)

Propagating these corrections to the background angular diameter distances, one finds, to linear order in vo/cv_{o}/c [Dalang2023]

dl\displaystyle d_{l} =d​[zl′]+DL​voc,\displaystyle=d[z_{l}^{\prime}]+D_{L}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\,, (4.7)
dl​s\displaystyle d_{ls} =d​[zl′,zs′]+DL​S​voc,\displaystyle=d[z_{l}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime}]+D_{LS}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\,, (4.8)

with the distance corrections

DL\displaystyle D_{L} =𝒏^′⋅(𝒗o−𝒗l)vo​(cH​[zl′]−d​[zl′]),\displaystyle=\frac{\bm{\hat{n}^{\prime}}\cdot(\bm{v}_{o}-\bm{v}_{l})}{v_{o}}\left(\frac{c}{H[z_{l}^{\prime}]}-d[z_{l}^{\prime}]\right)\,, (4.9)
DL​S\displaystyle D_{LS} =𝒏^′⋅(𝒗o−𝒗s)vo​(cH​[zs′]−d​[zl′,zs′])−cH​[zl′]​1+zl′1+zs′​𝒏^′⋅(𝒗o−𝒗l)vo.\displaystyle=\frac{\bm{\hat{n}^{\prime}}\cdot(\bm{v}_{o}-\bm{v}_{s})}{v_{o}}\left(\frac{c}{H[z_{s}^{\prime}]}-d[z_{l}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime}]\right)-\frac{c}{H[z_{l}^{\prime}]}\frac{1+z_{l}^{\prime}}{1+z_{s}^{\prime}}\frac{\bm{\hat{n}^{\prime}}\cdot(\bm{v}_{o}-\bm{v}_{l})}{v_{o}}\,. (4.10)

Here H​[z]=H0​E​(z)≡H0​Ωm​(1+z)3+(1−Ωm)H[z]=H_{0}E(z)\equiv H_{0}\sqrt{\Omega_{m}(1+z)^{3}+(1-\Omega_{m})} and 𝒗l\bm{v}_{l}, 𝒗s\bm{v}_{s} are the peculiar velocities of the lens and the source galaxy, respectively. Note that Eqs. (4.7) to (4.10) do not represent the observed angular diameter distances of a moving source and lens measured by a boosted observer, since those would also be affected by changes in the area distance, see e.g. [Cusin:2024git]. The aim of these expressions is to allow us to find the background angular diameter distances dld_{l} and dl​sd_{ls} (that enter in Eq. (4.1)) from the measured redshifts zl′z^{\prime}_{l} and zs′z^{\prime}_{s}, consistently accounting for the impact of velocities.

The relationship between the observable quantities zl′z_{l}^{\prime}, zs′z_{s}^{\prime}, σv\sigma_{v} and the predicted Einstein radius θE,spec′\theta^{\prime}_{E,\rm spec} is then given by

θE,spec′=4​π​σv2c2​d​[zl′,zs′]d​[zs′]​(1−vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′+DL​Sd​[zl′,zs′]​voc−DSd​[zs′]​voc),\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E,\rm spec}=\frac{4\pi\sigma_{v}^{2}}{c^{2}}\frac{d[z_{l}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime}]}{d[z_{s}^{\prime}]}\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}+\frac{D_{LS}}{d[z_{l}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}-\frac{D_{S}}{d[z_{s}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right), (4.11)

which can be compared to the observed θE′\theta_{E}^{\prime}. Contrary to the previous case, our aim here is not to compare each observed ring to the theoretical prediction for the mean ⟨θE′​(𝒏^′)⟩\langle\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})\rangle averaged over all source and lens redshifts in a given solid angle. Since we do know the redshifts of the sources and of the lenses, we can improve our analysis by comparing each ring with the theoretical prediction at the correct redshifts, given by Eq. (4.11). In this case, the likelihood becomes:

ln⁡ℒ2=−12​∑n=1N(θEn′−θEn,spec′−bVD)2δ​θEn2′+δ​θEn,spec2′+σsys,VD2−12​∑n=1Nln⁡[2​π​(δ​θEn2′+δ​θEn,spec2′+σsys,VD2)],\ln\mathcal{L}_{2}=-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\frac{\left(\theta^{\prime}_{E_{n}}-\theta^{\prime}_{E_{n},\rm spec}-b_{\rm VD}\right)^{2}}{\delta\theta^{{}^{\prime}2}_{E_{n}}+\delta\theta^{{}^{\prime}2}_{E_{n},\rm spec}+\sigma_{\rm sys,VD}^{2}}-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\ln\!\big[2\pi\big(\delta\theta^{{}^{\prime}2}_{E_{n}}+\delta\theta^{{}^{\prime}2}_{E_{n},\rm spec}+\sigma_{\rm sys,VD}^{2}\big)\big]\,, (4.12)

where we include a bias and a systematic error term, bVDb_{\rm VD} and σsys,VD\sigma_{\rm sys,VD}, which are fitted as free parameters. When simulating our lens sample, all peculiar velocity terms of the lens, the source, and the observer (Eqs. (4.9) and (4.10)) are included in the simulation. We assume that both the source and lens galaxies have peculiar velocities that are normally distributed, with random orientations and a standard deviation of 300300km​s−1{\,\mathrm{km}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}}. However, at inference, only the terms involving 𝒗o\bm{v}_{o} can be corrected for (since 𝒗o\bm{v}_{o} also enters into the dipole), while the terms involving 𝒗l\bm{v}_{l} and 𝒗s\bm{v}_{s} are not observable. This source of uncertainty, due to the random peculiar velocities of the lens and the source, is not expected to produce a dipole and is therefore absorbed into the bias and systematic error terms fitted to the data, which also absorb the redshift measurement error, assumed to be δ​zspec/zspec=0.001\delta z_{\rm spec}/z_{\rm spec}=0.001. This error term is small in comparison to the velocity dispersion measurement error.

The uncertainties on the Einstein radius derived from the velocity dispersion measurements, θEn,spec′\theta^{\prime}_{E_{n},\rm spec}, are propagated from the uncertainties on σv\sigma_{v}, which we take to be 7%7\%. This translates into a 14%14\% relative uncertainty on θEn,spec′\theta^{\prime}_{E_{n},\rm spec}. The 7%7\% uncertainty on σv\sigma_{v} itself combines a 5%5\% contribution from measurement errors, which is conservative compared to the most recent measurement [Knabel2025, TDCOSMO2025] and a 5%5\% contribution from modeling uncertainties, the latter reflecting possible departures of the mass profile from perfect sphericity or isothermality (see Section 5 for a discussion of this issue). The modeling uncertainties could, in theory, be reduced by a more complex axisymmetric Jeans modeling of the lenses instead of the simple SIS assumption, which is sufficient for our forecast.

4.2 Fundamental Plane

Although some spectroscopic follow-up observations of strong lenses are already planned within 4MOST [4SLSLS], the majority of the Euclid lens sample is unlikely to have spectroscopic velocity-dispersion measurements. Such measurements from ground-based telescopes are expected to be feasible only for the brightest lens galaxies in the sample. However, a larger fraction of the system will have spectroscopic source and lens redshift, or at least, reliable photometric redshifts. An estimation of the central velocity dispersion of the galaxy can be obtained through the Fundamental Plane (FP) relation, which relates the effective radius of the lens ReR_{e}, the central velocity dispersion σFP\sigma_{\mathrm{FP}} and the mean surface brightness within the effective radius IeI_{e} as

log⁡Re\displaystyle\log R_{e} =a​log⁡σFP+b​log⁡Ie+c.\displaystyle=a\log\sigma_{\mathrm{FP}}+b\log I_{e}+c\,. (4.13)

Only the angular size of the effective radius can be directly measured from the imaging data, but ReR_{e} can be straightforwardly derived from this measurement and from the (photometric) redshift of the lens, assuming a cosmological model. We assume a=1.177a=1.177, b=−0.793b=-0.793 and c=0.5c=0.5, obtained from the latest DESI measurement [Said2025]. Measurement errors on these parameters are ignored, as they lead to error much smaller than the intrinsic scatter of the FP. While IeI_{e} is invariant under a boost (as shown in the App. D), ReR_{e} is not. It can be computed from the observed subtended angle α′\alpha^{\prime} and lens redshift zl′z_{l}^{\prime}, which are affected by the boost, as

Re=α​dl=α′​d​[zl′]​(1+vo2​c​cos⁡(θcm′)+DLd​[zl′]​voc).\displaystyle R_{e}=\alpha d_{l}=\alpha^{\prime}d[z_{l}^{\prime}]\left(1+\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos(\theta_{\rm cm}^{\prime})+\frac{D_{L}}{d[z_{l}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\,. (4.14)

As a result, the velocity dispersion σFP\sigma_{\rm FP} determined from the FP is also affected by the peculiar velocities, as discussed in detail in App. D. On average, the observed Einstein angle θE′\theta^{\prime}_{\rm E} can be expressed as

θE,FP′=4​π​σFP′⁣2c2​d​[zl′,zs′]d​[zs′]​(1−12​voc​cos⁡(θcm′)+DL​Sd​[zl′,zs′]​voc−DSd​[zs′]​voc+2​Σv​voc),\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E,\rm FP}=\frac{4\pi\sigma_{\rm FP}^{\prime 2}}{c^{2}}\frac{d[z_{l}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime}]}{d[z_{s}^{\prime}]}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos(\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm})+\frac{D_{LS}}{d[z_{l}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}-\frac{D_{S}}{d[z_{s}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}+2\Sigma_{v}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\,, (4.15)

where σFP′\sigma_{\rm FP}^{\prime} and Σv\Sigma_{v} are defined as

σFP′\displaystyle\sigma_{\rm FP}^{\prime} =(10−c​α′​d​[zl′]​(Ie′)−b)1/a,\displaystyle=\left(10^{-c}\alpha^{\prime}d[z_{l}^{\prime}](I_{e}^{\prime})^{-b}\right)^{1/a}\,, (4.16)
Σv\displaystyle\Sigma_{v} =(12​a​cos⁡(θcm′)+DLa​d​[zl′]).\displaystyle=\left(\frac{1}{2a}\cos(\theta_{\rm cm}^{\prime})+\frac{D_{L}}{ad[z_{l}^{\prime}]}\right)\,. (4.17)

Eq. (4.15) can be used to infer vov_{o} from the observables θE′\theta^{\prime}_{\rm E}, zl′z_{l}^{\prime}, zs′z_{s}^{\prime}, α′\alpha^{\prime}, Ie′I_{e}^{\prime}, θcm′\theta_{\rm cm}^{\prime} and the parameters a,b,ca,b,c. In practice, it is, however, more convenient to define the likelihood in terms of the velocity dispersion rather than the Einstein radius, i.e., to compare σFP′\sigma_{\rm FP}^{\prime} with the velocity dispersion σSIS′\sigma^{\prime}_{\rm SIS} inferred from θE′\theta^{\prime}_{E} using Eq. (4.1). σFP′\sigma_{\rm FP}^{\prime} indeed has a large scatter, which is squared in Eq. (4.15), breaking down the assumption of a Gaussian likelihood. The likelihood function is hence defined as:

ln⁡ℒ3=−12​∑n=1N(σSIS,n′−σFP,n′−bFP)2δ​σSIS,n2+δ​σFP,n2+σsys,FP2−12​∑n=1Nln⁡[2​π​(δ​σSIS,n2+δ​σFP,n2+σsys,FP2)].\displaystyle\ln\mathcal{L}_{3}=-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\frac{\Big(\sigma^{\prime}_{{\rm SIS},n}-\sigma^{\prime}_{\mathrm{FP},n}-b_{\rm FP}\Big)^{2}}{\delta\sigma_{\mathrm{SIS},n}^{2}+\delta\sigma_{\mathrm{FP},n}^{2}+\sigma_{\rm sys,FP}^{2}}-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\ln\Big[2\pi\left(\delta\sigma_{\mathrm{SIS},n}^{2}+\delta\sigma_{\mathrm{FP},n}^{2}+\sigma_{\rm sys,FP}^{2}\right)\Big]. (4.18)

Details on how the uncertainties δ​σFP\delta\sigma_{\mathrm{FP}} are calculated can be found in App. C. Similarly to Eq. (4.12), we introduced a bias and systematic error term to account for model mismatch, peculiar velocity effects, and redshift measurement errors when converting Einstein angles to velocity dispersions. We treat separately the likelihood contributions from lenses with only photometric redshift estimates and those with spectroscopic redshift measurements for both source and lens, since these two subsamples require different bias and systematic error corrections owing to the differing precision of their redshift determinations.

All variables used in the simulation are listed in Table 2.

4.3 Euclid Forecast

We illustrate the gain in constraining power obtained by adding velocity-dispersion information for the “100k” lens sample, relative to the lensing-only case, in Figure 4. When only photometric redshift estimates for the lenses and sources are available, and the velocity dispersion is inferred using FP relations, the precision on vov_{o} improves by a factor of ∼1.5\sim 1.5. If spectroscopic redshift measurements for both the lens and the source are available, the constraints improve by a factor of ∼3\sim 3 compared to the lensing-only case. In the perfect scenario in which all lenses have spectroscopic redshifts for both the lens and the source, as well as direct spectroscopic measurements of the stellar velocity dispersion, the constraint improves by a factor of ∼10\sim 10. In the latter case, the inferred precision becomes sufficient to discriminate between the CMB-inferred value and the value suggested by the source number count analyzes at 5.6 - 5.8σ\sigma (depending on the fiducial value assumed for vov_{o}). All posterior distributions are reported in Table 4 as well as the exclusion significance of the alternative value of vov_{o}, averaged over five different realizations of the lens sample.

Finally, we assess the constraining power of several spectroscopic follow-up scenarios for the Euclid strong-lens sample. In a first, pessimistic scenario, we assume that only 15 00015\,000 lenses receive spectroscopic follow-up, either from large spectroscopic surveys such as DESI [DESI2016] or 4MOST [4MOSToverview, 4SLSLS], or from dedicated follow-up programs. While both redshift and stellar velocity-dispersion measurements are in principle accessible with fiber spectroscopy, the latter are significantly more challenging, as they require a higher signal-to-noise ratio to robustly detect the absorption features of the lens galaxy. As a result, even if a lens is targeted by DESI or 4MOST, a substantial fraction of systems are expected to yield redshift measurements only. In this pessimistic scenario, we therefore assume that velocity-dispersion measurements are successfully obtained for only a third of the spectroscopically targeted lenses, i.e. 5 0005\,000 lenses. The remaining lenses are assumed to have spectroscopic, photometric, or no redshift information, as summarized in Table 3.

In a more realistic scenario, we assume that the majority of lenses benefit from at least partial redshift information, either from follow-up spectroscopic observations, Euclid NISP slitless spectroscopy, or, at minimum, reliable photometric redshift estimates. In this case, we adopt a similar success rate for velocity-dispersion measurements, yielding 10 00010\,000 lenses with kinematic information out of 30 00030\,000 targeted lenses.

The third, ideal scenario corresponds to a dedicated spectroscopic survey of strong lenses, in which all lenses are followed up. We assume a velocity-dispersion measurement success rate of 50%, resulting in 50 00050\,000 lenses with stellar velocity-dispersion measurements and an additional 50 00050\,000 lenses with spectroscopic redshifts for both the lens and the source.

We show the posterior distribution contours for the three scenarios in Figure 5, assuming a fiducial peculiar velocity vo,true=1109.46v_{o,\rm true}=1109.46km​s−1{\,\mathrm{km}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}}. The median values, together with the 16th16^{\rm th} and 84th84^{\rm th} percentiles of the marginalized posterior distributions, are reported in Table 4 for a single realization of the lens samples. In addition, we quote the significance at which the alternative value of vov_{o} is excluded, averaged over five independent realizations for each scenario.

For a fiducial value of vo,true=1109.46v_{o,\rm true}=1109.46 km​s−1{\,\mathrm{km}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}}, all three spectroscopic follow-up scenarios exclude the CMB-inferred value of vov_{o} with a significance ranging from 1.7​σ1.7\sigma in the pessimistic scenario to 4.1​σ4.1\sigma in the ideal scenario. Conversely, when the CMB-inferred value of vov_{o} is adopted as the fiducial velocity, the source number-count value is excluded with a significance ranging from 1.3​σ1.3\sigma to 4.2​σ4.2\sigma across the same scenarios. These results indicate that strong-lensing measurements could provide valuable complementary information on the cosmic dipole tension in realistic spectroscopic follow-up configurations, with a potential discrimination between the two values of vov_{o} at the ∼2​σ\sim 2\sigma level. In the presence of a dedicated spectroscopic follow-up of strong lenses, however, this probe could make a decisive contribution to this debate, with a discriminating power exceeding 4​σ4\sigma.

5 Discussion

As shown in the previous section, strong-lensing measurements can reach a sufficient level of precision to discriminate between the CMB-inferred and source number-count values of vov_{o}, provided that adequate spectroscopic follow-up data are available for a large fraction of the Euclid strong-lens sample. This method would offer an independent test of the cosmic dipole, with systematic uncertainties that are fundamentally different from those affecting source number-count measurements.

Systematic errors:

A primary advantage of this method is that any bias in the absolute calibration of the FP, the measurement of the velocity dispersion, or the dynamical modeling of the galaxies (assumed to be an SIS in this paper) becomes irrelevant, provided such biases are uniform across the sky. However, the observer’s peculiar velocity directly modulates the FP observable ReR_{e} through two distinct relativistic effects. First, ReR_{e} is subject to angular aberration, analogous to that observed in the Einstein angle (Eq. (2.13)). Second, the observer’s peculiar velocity affects the conversion of the angular scale α\alpha of the galaxy into a physical scale ReR_{e} (in kpc) via the angular diameter distance, since the estimated redshift of the galaxy is impacted by the observer’s motion. Both effects are explicitly accounted for in the expression of the likelihood (Eq. (4.18)), ensuring that the inferred vov_{o} is unbiased.

Since the monopole and dipole are fitted simultaneously, extensive sky coverage is necessary to prevent biases in the inferred dipole amplitude. This is especially true for the lensing-only measurement (Sec. 3), as the Einstein angle is not compared against any absolute calibration, leading to a potential degeneracy between the monopole and dipole amplitudes if the sky coverage is insufficient. This effect can originate, for example, from galaxy clustering, which can modify both the average Einstein radius of the lenses and their number density on small scales, since denser regions of the Universe are expected to have a higher probability of lensing [Collett2016] and to produce larger Einstein radii. The area covered by the Euclid Wide Survey, which spans approximately 14 00014\,000 square degrees across both hemispheres, should, however, be largely sufficient to break this degeneracy.

Strong lensing selection function:

One of the main challenges in analyzing large samples of strong lenses is the characterization of the selection function, which can be particularly complex due to the diversity of lens-search algorithms used to identify the systems [Sonnenfeld2022, Sonnenfeld2023, Herle2024]. In Appendix B, we explicitly calculate the impact of the selection function on the dipole, for the lensing only probe. As we will see, the contribution is extremely small, less than 0.1%0.1\%, meaning that it is not necessary to model the selection function to infer the observer’s velocity. We consider two selections, the first one on the (magnified) flux of the source, and the second one on the size of the Einstein ring, such that the mean radius is given by

⟨θE′​(𝒏^′)⟩=\displaystyle\langle\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})\rangle= (dN(𝒏^′,θE′>θE⁣∗′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω′)−1\displaystyle\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},\theta^{\prime}_{E}>\theta^{\prime}_{E*},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}}\right)^{-1} (5.1)
×∫dzs′∫dzl′∫θE⁣∗′dθE′d​N​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′,θE′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′​d​θE′θE′(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′),\displaystyle\times\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\int_{\theta^{\prime}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta^{\prime}_{E},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}}\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})\,,

where F∗′F^{\prime}_{*} is the observed flux threshold of the source and θE⁣∗′\theta^{\prime}_{E*} is the observed size threshold. These selection effects have the potential to modulate the mean Einstein radius of the sample ⟨θE′⟩\langle\theta^{\prime}_{E}\rangle in the dipole direction, leading to a bias in vov_{o} if not properly accounted for. In the presence of kinematic data, however, the Einstein radii are compared on a per-lens basis, and therefore these selection effects, which affect the sample mean, become irrelevant.

Here, we consider hard cuts in flux and Einstein radius, but a smoother selection function would lead to even smaller biases. The flux threshold contributes to the dipole because a fixed observer-frame flux limit, F∗′F^{\prime}_{*}, translates into a direction-dependent threshold in the intrinsic luminosity, L∗​(𝒏^′,zs′)L_{*}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s}). This arises because the luminosity distance—which relates the observed flux to the intrinsic luminosity—is itself affected by the boost. As a consequence, the moving observer does not see the same number of rings in the direction of the boost as in the opposite direction. The same argument applies to the size threshold: a fixed threshold in the observer frame corresponds to different intrinsic sizes of the rings in different directions.

The detailed calculation can be found in Appendix B, where we found222As shown in the Appendix B, all functions are written in principle in terms of unboosted quantities. However, at the lowest order in vo/cv_{o}/c, we can replace the unboosted quantities (a priori unknown) with the boosted ones, such that functions are expressed in terms of observable quantities.

⟨θE′⟩\displaystyle\langle\theta^{\prime}_{E}\rangle =⟨θE⟩​(1−vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′)\displaystyle=\langle\theta_{E}\rangle\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right) (5.2)
+2​voc​cos⁡θcm′​∫dzs′​∫θE′⁣∗dθE′​(θE′−⟨θE⟩)​f1​(zs′,θE′,F′>F∗′)​x​(zs′,θE′,F∗′)\displaystyle+2\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int_{\theta_{E^{\prime}*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\theta^{\prime}_{E}-\langle\theta_{E}\rangle)f_{1}(z^{\prime}_{s},\theta^{\prime}_{E},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})x(z^{\prime}_{s},\theta^{\prime}_{E},F^{\prime}_{*})
+vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′​(⟨θE⟩−θE⁣∗′)​∫dzs′​∫dzl′​f2​(zs′,zl′,θE⁣∗′,F′>F∗′)​μ​(zs′,zl′,θE⁣∗′,F∗′).\displaystyle+\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}(\langle\theta_{E}\rangle-\theta^{\prime}_{E*})\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}f_{2}(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta^{\prime}_{E*},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})\mu(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta^{\prime}_{E*},F^{\prime}_{*})\,.

Here f1f_{1} is the distribution of detected rings (integrated over the redshift of the lenses) as a function of θE′\theta^{\prime}_{E}; and f2f_{2} is the cumulative distribution above the size threshold, see Eqs. (B.16) and (B.17). The relativistic magnification bias xx and the parameter μ\mu govern the amplitude of the threshold effects and are given by

x​(zs′,θE′,F∗′)\displaystyle x(z^{\prime}_{s},\theta^{\prime}_{E},F^{\prime}_{*}) =−∂ln∂ln⁡F∗′​(d​N​(zs′,θE′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω​d​zs′​d​θE′),\displaystyle=-\frac{\partial\ln}{\partial\ln F^{\prime}_{*}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(z^{\prime}_{s},\theta^{\prime}_{E},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}}\right)\,, (5.3)
μ​(zs′,zl′,θE⁣∗,L∗)\displaystyle\mu(z^{\prime}_{s},z_{l}^{\prime},\theta_{E*},L_{*}) =−∂ln∂ln⁡θE⁣∗′​(dN(zs′,zl′,θE′>θE⁣∗′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω​d​zs′​d​zl′).\displaystyle=-\frac{\partial\ln}{\partial\ln\theta^{\prime}_{E*}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta^{\prime}_{E}>\theta^{\prime}_{E*},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}}\right)\,. (5.4)

They represent the slope of the cumulative distribution of rings above the respective thresholds and therefore quantify how many more systems we detect in one direction because the flux and the size are boosted. For a complete sample, xx and μ\mu vanish exactly. In practice, these parameters can be estimated from the population of rings by binning the systems in flux and size, and measuring the slopes at different thresholds.

What is interesting from Eq. (5.2) is that the selection effects are strongly suppressed with respect to the case where we simply count objects. This is due to the fact that the mean ⟨θE′⟩\langle\theta^{\prime}_{E}\rangle is normalized by the number of detected rings. The modulation in the number of rings induced by threshold effects impacts both the numerator and the denominator in Eq. (5.1), and the two contributions partially cancel out, leading to a negligible impact. For example, the second term in Eq. (5.2), representing the effect of the selection in flux, is strongly suppressed by the (θE′−⟨θE⟩)(\theta^{\prime}_{E}-\langle\theta_{E}\rangle) term. If the parameter x​(zs′,θE′,F∗′)x(z_{s}^{\prime},\theta_{E}^{\prime},F_{*}^{\prime}) was independent of zs′z_{s}^{\prime} and θE′\theta_{E}^{\prime}, then this contribution would be exactly zero since it would not change the sample mean ⟨θE⟩\langle\theta_{E}\rangle. In practice, this is not the case, since the observed source flux is related to zs′z_{s}^{\prime} and θE′\theta_{E}^{\prime} through the lensing magnification. However, this means that the surviving contribution is proportional to the change in the mean sample induced by the flux selection. We have computed this effect by evaluating xx from our sample and computing the second line of Eq. (5.2), and we have found that it generates a negligible contribution to the dipole that is <10−6<10^{-6} of the main effect.

The contribution from the size threshold, encoded in the third line of Eq. (5.2), is also suppressed, but this time by a factor (⟨θE⟩−θE⁣∗′)(\langle\theta_{E}\rangle-\theta^{\prime}_{E*}). In this case, there is a balance between this factor and the parameter μ\mu. If most systems have sizes well above the threshold θE⁣∗′\theta^{\prime}_{E*}, then (⟨θE⟩−θE⁣∗′)(\langle\theta_{E}\rangle-\theta^{\prime}_{E*}) will be large, but the effect will be strongly suppressed by μ\mu, which tends to zero in the limit where there are no rings below the threshold. Conversely, if most rings lie close to the threshold, then μ\mu can be large, but the effect is suppressed by (⟨θE⟩−θE⁣∗′)(\langle\theta_{E}\rangle-\theta^{\prime}_{E*}), which becomes small for rings near the threshold. We estimate the magnitude of this effect by evaluating μ\mu from our sample, assuming a threshold of θE⁣∗′=0.2′′\theta^{\prime}_{E*}=0.2\hbox{${}^{\prime\prime}$}, and computing the third line of Eq. (5.2). We find that this contribution is of order 0.05%0.05\% of the main effect. Although significantly larger than the contribution from the flux threshold, it remains completely negligible compared to the main effect.

This demonstrates that the dipole in the Einstein rings is almost insensitive to the strong lensing selection function, provided it is uniform across the sky. As such, it provides a very robust way of measuring the observer velocity. In other words, neglecting these threshold effects would bias the measurement of the observer velocity by 0.05%0.05\% at most, largely below the statistical uncertainty.

Limitations of the model:

In this study, we developed a simple Bayesian framework to assess whether a dipolar modulation of the Einstein angle induced by the observer’s peculiar velocity can be measured from future, yet realistic, samples of strong lenses. Although our lens population model accounts for the selection function of the lenses, we assumed simple Gaussian probability distributions for the measurements of the Einstein angle, stellar velocity dispersion, and redshifts, as well as a trivariate Gaussian model for the FP. However, when propagating these uncertainties through the likelihood (Eq. (4.18)), the Gaussian approximation proved insufficient. To address this limitation, we introduced a bias term and an additional systematic error term, which were sufficient to capture the leading-order deviations from Gaussianity and to recover unbiased measurements of vov_{o}, lol_{o}, and bob_{o}. A full hierarchical Bayesian inference framework will be required to model these effects more rigorously. Such an approach would allow the measurement uncertainties and intrinsic scatter of the lens and source populations to be modeled consistently at the population level, rather than being absorbed into effective bias and systematic error terms. This may further improve the precision and robustness of the dipole measurement. We leave such an analysis for future work.

Finally, a main limitation of this study is that we ignore the impact of ellipticity in the mass distribution of the lensing galaxies and cosmic shear. The ellipticity of the Einstein ring contains additional directional information that could help constrain the dipole direction while simultaneously acting as a source of noise when combining lensing and kinematic information. In the lensing-only case, we emphasize that we use the azimuthally averaged Einstein radius as our primary observable. This quantity is sensitive to the enclosed mass of the galaxy, the angular-diameter–distance ratio, and cosmic convergence, which is expected to average out on large scales. In the lensing+kinematic case, ellipticity in the mass distribution of the lens galaxy introduces additional scatter in the predicted central velocity dispersion. Departures from isothermality in the mass profile would produce a similar effect. In this work, we account for both sources of uncertainty by adopting a 5%5\% modeling error in addition to the 5%5\% measurement error. Future improvements of this technique may include full dynamical modeling of the lenses as well as shear and convergence correction from weak lensing maps, allowing one to exploit the directional information encoded in the elliptical deformation of the Einstein ring. The aberration due to the observer’s velocity might appear as a dipole component in the line-of-sight shear of strong gravitational lenses, proposed by [Fleury2026].

6 Conclusion

In this work, we present a novel method that could provide a fully-independent way of measuring the kinematic cosmic dipole, based on angle measurement rather than the traditional number counts technique, which relies on flux measurements. Our main findings are the following:

  • •

    A detection of the dipolar modulation of the Einstein radii of strong lenses is possible using the lensing information contained in Euclid images alone, but only in the case where the velocity is as large as inferred from the number-count analyses.

  • •

    The inclusion of kinematic information, either through spectroscopic velocity-dispersion measurements or via FP relations, significantly improves the constraining power of the method. Even in the absence of spectroscopic redshift information, the combination of FP relations and photometric redshifts leads to an improvement of ∼50\sim 50% compared to lensing-only constraints.

  • •

    Already existing or planned spectroscopic follow-up observations from 4MOST [4SLSLS] and DESI [e.g., Huang2025] should yield a discriminating power at the level of ∼2​σ\sim 2\sigma between the value of vov_{o} measured from the CMB and that measured from sources number counts.

  • •

    A hypothetical dedicated strong-lensing spectroscopic follow-up of the full Euclid sample could provide a decisive contribution to this debate, with a discriminating power reaching ∼4​σ\sim 4\sigma.

  • •

    The method is mostly insensitive to the lens selection function, provided it is spatially uniform over the survey footprint. Similarly, any bias in the lens mass modeling or in the dynamical modeling of the lenses can be self-calibrated from the data and does not affect the measurement of vov_{o}, as long as these biases are constant across the sky.

The recent discovery of hundreds of new strong-lens systems in the Euclid Q1 data highlights the potential to increase the current sample of known lenses by two orders of magnitude with the full Euclid survey. With such a large sample, we propose a direct cosmological test that can be performed using the imaging survey data, complemented by modest spectroscopic follow-up from Euclid NISP, DESI, or 4MOST.

Acknowledgments

MM acknowledges support from the Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF) through return CH grant P5R5PT_225598 and Ambizione grant PZ00P2_223738. This work has received funding from the European Research Council (ERC) under the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation program (LensEra: grant agreement No 945536). TEC is funded by the Royal Society through a University Research Fellowship. CB acknowledges support from the Swiss National Science Foundation (SNSF).

Appendix A Calculation of the mean Einstein radius integrated over redshifts

To calculate Eq. (2.9), we need to compute the Einstein angle and the number of sources as functions of the measured redshifts zs′z^{\prime}_{s} and zl′z^{\prime}_{l}. These redshifts are affected by the observer velocity vov_{o} through

1+zs′\displaystyle 1+z^{\prime}_{s} =(1+zs)​(1−voc​cos⁡θcm′),\displaystyle=(1+z_{s})\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)\,, (A.1)
1+zl′\displaystyle 1+z^{\prime}_{l} =(1+zl)​(1−voc​cos⁡θcm′).\displaystyle=(1+z_{l})\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)\,. (A.2)

The component of θE′\theta^{\prime}_{E} along the 𝒆1′\bm{e}^{\prime}_{1} direction at fixed zs′z^{\prime}_{s} and zl′z^{\prime}_{l} is given by

θE​1′​(δ′,𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)=θE​1′​(δ′,𝒏^′,r′​(zs′,𝒏^′),r′​(zl′,𝒏^′)),\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E1}(\delta^{\prime},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})=\theta^{\prime}_{E1}(\delta^{\prime},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},r^{\prime}(z^{\prime}_{s},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime}),r^{\prime}(z^{\prime}_{l},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime}))\,, (A.3)

where r′​(zs′,𝒏^′)r^{\prime}(z^{\prime}_{s},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime}) is the comoving distance associated with the boosted redshift zs′z^{\prime}_{s}, which depends on direction 𝒏^′\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime} through the impact of the boost on the redshift. We split this distance into an isotropic part and a perturbation

r′​(zs′,𝒏^′)=r​(zs′)+δ​r​(zs′,𝒏^′).\displaystyle r^{\prime}(z^{\prime}_{s},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})=r(z^{\prime}_{s})+\delta r(z^{\prime}_{s},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})\,. (A.4)

As shown in [Dalang:2021ruy], the perturbation in the distance is given by

δ​r​(zs′,𝒏^′)=1ℋ​(zs′)​voc​cos⁡θcm′.\displaystyle\delta r(z^{\prime}_{s},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})=\frac{1}{{\cal H}(z^{\prime}_{s})}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,. (A.5)

Doing the same split for the redshift of the lens, we can insert them into (A.3) and Taylor expand at linear order in vo/cv_{o}/c

θE​1′​(δ′,𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)=θE​(δ′,r​(zs′),r​(zl′))​cos⁡δ′+(∂θE∂rs​ℋ​(zs′)+∂θE∂rl​ℋ​(zl′))​cos⁡δ′​voc​cos⁡θcm′.\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E1}(\delta^{\prime},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})=\theta_{E}(\delta^{\prime},r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\cos\delta^{\prime}+\left(\frac{\partial\theta_{E}}{\partial r_{s}{\cal H}(z^{\prime}_{s})}+\frac{\partial\theta_{E}}{\partial r_{l}{\cal H}(z^{\prime}_{l})}\right)\cos\delta^{\prime}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,. (A.6)

From this we see that even though the angles along 𝒆1′\bm{e}^{\prime}_{1} are not aberrated, the fact that the redshifts of the lens and of the source are affected by the boost has an impact on θE​1′\theta^{\prime}_{E1}. Physically, this is due to the fact that a source at fixed redshift zs′z^{\prime}_{s} is not at the same physical distance from the observer if it is in the direction of the boost or if it is in the opposite direction. Since the Einstein radius depends on the distance of the source, this impacts the observed θE​1′\theta^{\prime}_{E1}. The calculation for θE​2′\theta^{\prime}_{E2} is similar, with the effect from the redshift added to the effect of aberration

θE​2′​(δ′,𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)=\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E2}(\delta^{\prime},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})= θE​(δ′,r​(zs′),r​(zl′))​sin⁡δ′​(1−voc​cos⁡θcm′)\displaystyle\theta_{E}(\delta^{\prime},r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\sin\delta^{\prime}\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right) (A.7)
+(∂θE∂rs​ℋ​(zs′)+∂θE∂rl​ℋ​(zl′))​sin⁡δ′​voc​cos⁡θcm′.\displaystyle+\left(\frac{\partial\theta_{E}}{\partial r_{s}{\cal H}(z^{\prime}_{s})}+\frac{\partial\theta_{E}}{\partial r_{l}{\cal H}(z^{\prime}_{l})}\right)\sin\delta^{\prime}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,.

Computing θE′​(δ′,𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\delta^{\prime},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime},z_{l}^{\prime}) and averaging over δ′\delta^{\prime}, we find

θE′​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)=\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})= θE​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))​(1−vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′)+((1+zz)​∂θE∂zs+(1+zl)​∂θE∂zl)​voc​cos⁡θcm′,\displaystyle\theta_{E}(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)+\left((1+z_{z})\frac{\partial\theta_{E}}{\partial z_{s}}+(1+z_{l})\frac{\partial\theta_{E}}{\partial z_{l}}\right)\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,, (A.8)

where we have used that ∂/∂r=ℋ​(1+z)​∂/∂z\partial/\partial r={\cal H}(1+z)\partial/\partial z.

To obtain the mean Einstein radius in a solid angle d​Ω′\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}, we need to weight it by the number of systems at fixed zs′,zl′z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l}. We have

d​N​(zs′,zl′,𝒏^′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm{d}N(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}} =d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​(1−δ​ΩΩ−δ​zs1+zs−δ​zl1+zl)\displaystyle=\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\left(1-\frac{\delta\Omega}{\Omega}-\frac{\delta z_{s}}{1+z_{s}}-\frac{\delta z_{l}}{1+z_{l}}\right)
+∂∂rs​(d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl)​δ​rs+∂∂rl​(d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl)​δ​rl\displaystyle+\frac{\partial}{\partial r_{s}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\right)\delta r_{s}+\frac{\partial}{\partial r_{l}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\right)\delta r_{l}
=d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​(1+4​voc​cos⁡θcm′)\displaystyle=\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\left(1+4\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)
+[(1+zs)​∂∂zs​(d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl)+(1+zl)​∂∂zl​(d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl)]​voc​cos⁡θcm′.\displaystyle+\left[(1+z_{s})\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{s}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\right)+(1+z_{l})\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{l}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\right)\right]\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,. (A.9)

Multiplying Eq. (A.9) with (A.8) and using that

∂∂zs​(d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl)​(1+zs)+[∂θE∂rs​ℋ​(zs′)+1]​d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl=∂∂zs​[d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​(1+zs)​θE]\displaystyle\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{s}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\right)(1+z_{s})+\left[\frac{\partial\theta_{E}}{\partial r_{s}{\cal H}(z^{\prime}_{s})}+1\right]\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}=\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{s}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}(1+z_{s})\theta_{E}\right] (A.10)

we obtain

∫dzs′​∫dzl′​θE′​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)​d​N​(zs′,zl′,𝒏^′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′\displaystyle\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{l})\frac{\mathrm{d}N(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}} (A.11)
=∫dzs′​∫dzl′​d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​θE​(1+2​voc​cos⁡θcm′−vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′)\displaystyle=\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\theta_{E}\left(1+2\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)
+∫dzs′​∫dzl′​{∂∂zs​[d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​(1+zs)​θE]+∂∂zl​[d​Nd​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​(1+zl)​θE]}​voc​cos⁡θcm′.\displaystyle+\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\left\{\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{s}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}(1+z_{s})\theta_{E}\right]+\frac{\partial}{\partial z_{l}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}N}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}(1+z_{l})\theta_{E}\right]\right\}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,.

The terms in the last line can directly be integrated, giving rise to boundary terms that exactly vanish since the number of rings at redshift 0 and at infinity is zero. The only effect that survives is therefore the effect of aberration, which modifies both the number of rings (second term in the second line of (A.11)) and the Einstein radius (third term in the second line of (A.11)). A similar calculation can be performed for the denominator of Eq. (2.9), d​N​(𝒏^′)/d​Ω′dN(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})/d\Omega^{\prime}, leading to

d​N​(𝒏^′)d​Ω′=∫dzs′​∫dzl′​d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​θE​(1+2​voc​cos⁡θcm′).\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}}=\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\theta_{E}\left(1+2\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)\,. (A.12)

Taking the ratio of Eqs. (A.11) and (A.12) , we obtain for the mean

⟨θE′​(𝒏^′)⟩=(1−vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′)​∫dzs′​∫dzl′​f​(zs′,zl′)​θE​(zs′,zl′),\displaystyle\langle\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})\rangle=\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}f(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})\theta_{E}(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})\,, (A.13)

with f​(zs′,zl′)f(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l}) given in Eq. (2.15).

Appendix B Impact of the selection function on the dipole

We compute here the impact of selection in flux and size on the dipole. We work in redshift space, but as we will see, we would obtain the same result using comoving distances, once the signal is integrated from 0 to ∞\infty.

The mean Einstein radius, averaged over sources with a flux larger than F∗′F^{\prime}_{*} and a size larger than θE⁣∗′\theta^{\prime}_{E*}, is given by

⟨θE′​(𝒏^′)⟩=\displaystyle\langle\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime})\rangle= (dN(𝒏^′,θE′>θE⁣∗′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω′)−1\displaystyle\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},\theta^{\prime}_{E}>\theta^{\prime}_{E*},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}}\right)^{-1} (B.1)
×∫dzs′∫dzl′∫θE⁣∗′dθE′d​N​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′,θE′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′​d​θE′θE′(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′).\displaystyle\times\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\int_{\theta^{\prime}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta^{\prime}_{E},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}}\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})\,.

In principle, we could also add a threshold on the flux of the lens, but this is irrelevant since the vast majority of sources for which the ring is detected have a lens that is also detected.

The observed flux is related to the intrinsic source luminosity LL through the luminosity distance

F′=L4​π​dL2′.\displaystyle F^{\prime}=\frac{L}{4\pi d^{{}^{\prime}2}_{L}}\,. (B.2)

The observed flux threshold F∗′F_{*}^{\prime} is, by construction, independent of direction. However, since the luminosity distance depends on direction through the boost, the intrinsic luminosity threshold also depends on direction

L∗​(r​(zs′))+δ​L∗​(𝒏^′,zs′)=4​π​dL2​(1+2​δ​dLdL)​F∗′=4​π​dL2​(1+2​vo​cos⁡θcm′c​ℋ​r)​F∗′,\displaystyle L_{*}(r(z_{s}^{\prime}))+\delta L_{*}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s})=4\pi d_{L}^{2}\left(1+2\frac{\delta d_{L}}{d_{L}}\right)F_{*}^{\prime}=4\pi d_{L}^{2}\left(1+2\frac{v_{o}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}}{c{\cal H}r}\right)F_{*}^{\prime}\,, (B.3)

where the luminosity distance fluctuation has been computed in [Bonvin:2005ps]. We will therefore detect a different number of rings above the flux threshold in the direction of the boost than in the opposite direction. If this effect were independent of the distance, it would affect the numerator and the denominator of (B.1) in the same way, and it would cancel. However, as we will see, this is not the case, and there is a non-zero contribution.

Similarly to the flux, we can relate the observed Einstein radius to its intrinsic size (which depends only on the mass of the lens) by factoring out the dependence on angular diameter distances

θE′=θEint​(1−vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′)​g​(zs′,zl′),\displaystyle\theta_{E}^{\prime}=\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)g(z_{s}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{l})\,, (B.4)

where g​(zs′,zl′)=dl​s​(zs′,zl′)/ds​(zs′)g(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})=d_{ls}(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})/d_{s}(z^{\prime}_{s}). As for the luminosity, since the observed size threshold is independent of direction, the intrinsic size threshold acquires a dependence on 𝒏^′\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime} through the boost

θE⁣∗int​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))+δ​θE⁣∗int​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)=θE⁣∗′g​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))​(1+vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′−∂g∂rs​δ​rsg−∂g∂rl​δ​rlg).\displaystyle\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))+\delta\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l})=\frac{\theta^{\prime}_{E*}}{g(r(z_{s}^{\prime}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))}\left(1+\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}-\frac{\partial g}{\partial r_{s}}\frac{\delta r_{s}}{g}-\frac{\partial g}{\partial r_{l}}\frac{\delta r_{l}}{g}\right)\,. (B.5)

We then have

∫θE⁣∗′dθE′​d​N​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′,θE′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′​d​θE′​θE′=∫θE⁣∗int+δ​θE⁣∗intdθEint​d​N​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′,θEint,L>L∗+δ​L∗)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′​d​θEint​θE′\displaystyle\int_{\theta^{\prime}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta^{\prime}_{E},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}}\theta_{E}^{\prime}=\int_{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}+\delta\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*}+\delta L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}\theta_{E}^{\prime}
=∫θE⁣∗intdθEintθEint{d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEintg(r(zs′),r(zl′))(1+32voccosθcm′)\displaystyle=\int_{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\Bigg\{\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\left(1+\frac{3}{2}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)
+∂∂zs′​[d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint​(1+zs′)​g​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))]​voc​cos⁡θcm′\displaystyle\quad+\frac{\partial}{\partial z^{\prime}_{s}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}(1+z^{\prime}_{s})g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\right]\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}
+∂∂zl′​[d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint​(1+zl′)​g​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))]​voc​cos⁡θcm′\displaystyle\quad+\frac{\partial}{\partial z^{\prime}_{l}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}(1+z^{\prime}_{l})g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\right]\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}
+∂∂L∗[d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint]g(r(zs′),r(zl′))δL∗}\displaystyle\quad+\frac{\partial}{\partial L_{*}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}\right]g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\delta L_{*}\Bigg\}
+∂∂θE⁣∗int​[∫θE⁣∗intdθEint​θEint​d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint​g​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))]​δ​θE⁣∗int.\displaystyle+\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\left[\int_{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\right]\delta\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}\,. (B.6)

As in the case without threshold effects in Appendix A, we want to group the terms in a total derivative that vanishes at the boundary. The difference here is that both L∗L_{*} and θE⁣∗int\theta^{\rm int}_{E*} depend on redshifts. L∗L_{*} depends only on the redshift of the source, while θE⁣∗int\theta^{\rm int}_{E*} depends on both redshifts. As a consequence, the total derivative acquires additional terms. More precisely the total derivative over zs′z_{s}^{\prime} is given by

dd​zs′​{∫θE⁣∗intdθEint​θEint​d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint​(1+zs′)​g​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))}​voc​cos⁡θcm′\displaystyle\frac{d}{dz^{\prime}_{s}}\Bigg\{\int_{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}(1+z^{\prime}_{s})g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\Bigg\}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm} (B.7)
