=∫θE⁣∗intdθEintθEint{∂∂zs′[d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint(1+zs′)g(r(zs′),r(zl′))]\displaystyle=\int_{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\Bigg\{\frac{\partial}{\partial z^{\prime}_{s}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}(1+z^{\prime}_{s})g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\right]
+∂∂L∗[d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint]g(r(zs′),r(zl′))(1+zs′)d​L∗d​zs′}voccosθ′cm\displaystyle+\frac{\partial}{\partial L_{*}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}\right]g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))(1+z^{\prime}_{s})\frac{\mathrm{d}L_{*}}{\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}}\Bigg\}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}
+∂∂θE⁣∗int​[∫θE⁣∗intdθEint​θEint​d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint]​g​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))​(1+zs′)​d​θE⁣∗intd​zs′​voc​cos⁡θcm′.\displaystyle+\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\left[\int_{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}\right]g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))(1+z^{\prime}_{s})\frac{\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}{\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,.

A similar expression can be derived for zl′z^{\prime}_{l}, without the contribution from the third line since L∗L_{*}, which is the intrinsic luminosity of the source, does not depend on the redshift of the lens. Using Eq. (B.7) in Eq. (B.6) and integrating over redshifts, we find

∫dzs′​∫dzl′​∫θE⁣∗′dθE′​d​N​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′,θE′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′​d​θE′​θE′​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)\displaystyle\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\int_{\theta^{\prime}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta^{\prime}_{E},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}}\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l}) (B.8)
=∫dzs′∫dzl′{∫θE⁣∗intdθEintθEint[d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEintg(r(zs′),r(zl′))(1+32voccosθcm′)\displaystyle=\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\Bigg\{\int_{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\Bigg[\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\left(1+\frac{3}{2}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)
+∂∂L∗(d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint)g(r(zs′),r(zl′))(δL∗−(1+zs′)d​L∗d​zs′voccosθcm′)]\displaystyle+\frac{\partial}{\partial L_{*}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}\right)g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\left(\delta L_{*}-(1+z^{\prime}_{s})\frac{\mathrm{d}L_{*}}{\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)\Bigg]
+∂∂θE⁣∗int​(∫θE⁣∗intdθEint​θEint​d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint)\displaystyle+\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\left(\int_{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}\right)
×g(r(zs′),r(zl′))[δθE⁣∗int−((1+zs′)d​θE⁣∗intd​zs′+(1+zl′)d​θE⁣∗intd​zl′)voccosθcm′]},\displaystyle\quad\times g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\left[\delta\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}-\left((1+z^{\prime}_{s})\frac{\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}{\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}}+(1+z^{\prime}_{l})\frac{\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}{\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}}\right)\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right]\Bigg\}\,,

where we have used that the total derivatives over zs′z^{\prime}_{s} and zl′z^{\prime}_{l} cancel when integrated over all redshifts, since the number of rings at redshift 0 and infinity is zero. The luminosity perturbation and its redshift derivative can be directly computed from Eq. (B.3)

δ​L∗\displaystyle\delta L_{*} =2​L∗ℋ​r​voc​cos⁡θcm′,\displaystyle=\frac{2L_{*}}{{\cal H}r}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,, (B.9)
(1+zs′)​d​L∗d​zs′\displaystyle(1+z^{\prime}_{s})\frac{\mathrm{d}L_{*}}{\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}} =2​L∗​(1+1rs​ℋ).\displaystyle=2L_{*}\left(1+\frac{1}{r_{s}{\cal H}}\right)\,. (B.10)

Similarly, the intrinsic size perturbation and its redshift derivatives can be computed from Eq. (B.5)

δ​θE⁣∗int=θE⁣∗int​(vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′−∂g∂rs​δ​rsg−∂g∂rl​δ​rlg),\displaystyle\delta\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}=\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}\left(\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}-\frac{\partial g}{\partial r_{s}}\frac{\delta r_{s}}{g}-\frac{\partial g}{\partial r_{l}}\frac{\delta r_{l}}{g}\right)\,, (B.11)
(1+zs′)​d​θE⁣∗intd​zs′+(1+zl′)​d​θE⁣∗intd​zl′=−θE⁣∗intg​(∂g∂zs′+∂g∂zl′).\displaystyle(1+z^{\prime}_{s})\frac{\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}{\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}}+(1+z^{\prime}_{l})\frac{\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}{\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}}=-\frac{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}{g}\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial z^{\prime}_{s}}+\frac{\partial g}{\partial z^{\prime}_{l}}\right)\,. (B.12)

With this, Eq. (B.7) becomes

∫dzs′​∫dzl′​∫θE⁣∗′dθE′​d​N​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′,θE′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω′​d​zs′​d​zl′​d​θE′​θE′​(𝒏^′,zs′,zl′)\displaystyle\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\int_{\theta^{\prime}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta^{\prime}_{E},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}}\theta^{\prime}_{E}(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l}) (B.13)
=∫dzs′∫dzl′∫θE⁣∗intdθEintθEint[d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEintg(r(zs′),r(zl′))(1+32voccosθcm′)\displaystyle=\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\int_{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\Bigg[\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\left(1+\frac{3}{2}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)
−2∂∂L∗(d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint)g(r(zs′),r(zl′))L∗voccosθcm′]\displaystyle-2\frac{\partial}{\partial L_{*}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}\right)g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))L_{*}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\Bigg]
−∫dzs′​∫dzl′​θE⁣∗int​d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θE⁣∗int,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint​g​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′))​θE⁣∗int​vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′.\displaystyle-\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\,\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E*},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}g(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}))\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,.

The last two lines are new contributions generated by the threshold effects: since the boost impacts the observed flux and the observed size of the Einstein ring, we see a different number of events in the direction of the boost and in the opposite direction, which impacts the integral over redshifts.

To obtain the mean Einstein radius, we need, however, to divide Eq. (B.13) by the number of events observed in the direction 𝒏^′\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime}. Following the same steps as for Eq. (B.13), we find

dN(𝒏^′,θE′>θE⁣∗′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω′\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm{d}N(\bm{\hat{n}}^{\prime},\theta^{\prime}_{E}>\theta^{\prime}_{E*},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega^{\prime}} (B.14)
=∫dzs′∫dzl′∫θE⁣∗intdθEint[d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint(1+2voccosθcm′)\displaystyle=\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\int_{\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}\Bigg[\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}\left(1+2\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right)
−2∂∂L∗(d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θEint,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint)L∗voccosθcm′]\displaystyle-2\frac{\partial}{\partial L_{*}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}\right)L_{*}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\Bigg]
−∫dzs′​∫dzl′​d​N​(r​(zs′),r​(zl′),θE⁣∗int,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl​d​θEint​θE⁣∗int​vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′.\displaystyle-\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta^{\rm int}_{E*},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\rm int}_{E}}\theta^{\rm int}_{E*}\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\,.

We then rewrite Eqs. (B.13) and (B.14) in terms of the Einstein radius θE=g​(r​(zs′,r​(zl′)))​θEint\theta_{E}=g(r(z_{s}^{\prime},r(z_{l}^{\prime})))\theta^{\rm int}_{E}, and we take the ratio. This gives the final result

⟨θE′⟩\displaystyle\langle\theta^{\prime}_{E}\rangle =⟨θE⟩​(1−vo2​c​cos⁡θcm′)\displaystyle=\langle\theta_{E}\rangle\left(1-\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\right) (B.15)
+2​voc​cos⁡θcm′​∫dzs′​∫θE⁣∗dθE​(θE−⟨θE⟩)​f1​(zs′,θE,L>L∗)​x​(zs′,θE,L∗)\displaystyle+2\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int_{\theta_{E*}}\mathrm{d}\theta_{E}(\theta_{E}-\langle\theta_{E}\rangle)f_{1}(z^{\prime}_{s},\theta_{E},L>L_{*})x(z^{\prime}_{s},\theta_{E},L_{*})
+vo2​ccosθcm′(⟨θE⟩−θE⁣∗)∫dzs′∫dzl′f2(zs′,zl′,θE>θE⁣∗,L>L∗)μ(zs′,zl′,θE⁣∗,L∗).\displaystyle+\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm}(\langle\theta_{E}\rangle-\theta_{E*})\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\int\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{l}f_{2}(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta_{E}>\theta_{E*},L>L_{*})\mu(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta_{E*},L_{*})\,.

Here f1f_{1} is the distribution of systems, already integrated over the redshifts of the lenses (which do not impact the flux threshold)

f1​(zs′,θE,L>L∗)=(d​N​(θE>θE⁣∗,L>L∗)d​Ω)−1​d​N​(r​(zs′),θE,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​θE,\displaystyle f_{1}(z^{\prime}_{s},\theta_{E},L>L_{*})=\left(\frac{dN(\theta_{E}>\theta_{E*},L>L_{*})}{d\Omega}\right)^{-1}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),\theta_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}\theta_{E}}\,, (B.16)

and f2f_{2} is the cumulative distribution with sizes above the threshold

f2(zs′,zl′,θE>θE⁣∗,L>L∗)=(d​N​(θE>θE⁣∗,L>L∗)d​Ω)−1dN(r(zs′),r(zl′),θE>θE⁣∗,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d.\displaystyle f_{2}(z^{\prime}_{s},z^{\prime}_{l},\theta_{E}>\theta_{E*},L>L_{*})=\left(\frac{dN(\theta_{E}>\theta_{E*},L>L_{*})}{d\Omega}\right)^{-1}\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta_{E}>\theta_{E*},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}}\,. (B.17)

These two distributions are unboosted quantities, which depend on the unboosted distance rr, the unboosted angle θE\theta_{E}, and the intrinsic unboosted luminosity threshold L∗L_{*}. In practice, however, to predict the amplitude of the second and third lines in Eq. (B.15), we can directly use the measured mean redshift distribution as a function of the observed redshifts, the observed angle θE′\theta^{\prime}_{E}, and the observed flux threshold F′>F∗′F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*}, since f1f_{1} and f2f_{2} multiply already vo/cv_{o}/c.

The second line in Eq. (B.15) encodes the impact of the flux threshold on the mean Einstein ring. It depends on the so-called (relativistic) magnification bias, i.e., the slope of the cumulative number of systems with a flux larger than the flux threshold

x​(zs′,θE,L∗)≡−∂ln∂ln⁡L∗​(d​N​(r​(zs′),θE,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​θE)=−∂ln∂ln⁡F∗′​(d​N​(zs′,θE′,F′>F∗′)d​Ω​d​zs′​d​θE′),\displaystyle x(z^{\prime}_{s},\theta_{E},L_{*})\equiv-\frac{\partial\ln}{\partial\ln L_{*}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),\theta_{E},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}\theta_{E}}\right)=-\frac{\partial\ln}{\partial\ln F^{\prime}_{*}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(z^{\prime}_{s},\theta^{\prime}_{E},F^{\prime}>F^{\prime}_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z^{\prime}_{s}\mathrm{d}\theta^{\prime}_{E}}\right)\,, (B.18)

where in the second equality we have used the fact that xx already multiplies vo/cv_{o}/c.

Finally, the third line in Eq. (B.15) encodes the impact of the size threshold. It depends on the slope of the cumulative number of systems with a size larger than θE⁣∗\theta_{E*}

μ​(zs′,zl′,θE⁣∗,L∗)≡−∂ln∂ln⁡θE⁣∗​(dN(r(zs′),r(zl′),θE>θE⁣∗,L>L∗)d​Ω​d​zs​d​zl).\displaystyle\mu(z^{\prime}_{s},z_{l}^{\prime},\theta_{E*},L_{*})\equiv-\frac{\partial\ln}{\partial\ln\theta_{E*}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}N(r(z^{\prime}_{s}),r(z^{\prime}_{l}),\theta_{E}>\theta_{E*},L>L_{*})}{\mathrm{d}\Omega\mathrm{d}z_{s}\mathrm{d}z_{l}}\right)\,. (B.19)

As for the magnification bias, unboosted quantities can be replaced by the measured ones in the definition of μ\mu.

Appendix C Simulation of the Fundamental Plane observables

We define the FP logarithmic variables:

𝒙≡(r,s,i)≡(log⁡Re,log⁡σFP,log⁡Ie),\bm{x}\equiv(r,\,s,\,i)\equiv\left(\log R_{e},\ \log\sigma_{\mathrm{FP}},\ \log I_{e}\right), (C.1)

and we model the intrinsic distribution of galaxies as a tri-variate Gaussian, following e.g. [Colless2001b]:

P​(𝒙)=𝒩​(𝝁,𝚺),P(\bm{x})=\mathcal{N}(\bm{\mu},\,\bm{\Sigma})\,, (C.2)

with mean

𝝁=(r¯,s¯,i¯),\bm{\mu}=(\bar{r},\ \bar{s},\ \bar{i})\,, (C.3)

and covariance matrix 𝚺\bm{\Sigma}.

The covariance matrix is constructed from the orthonormal eigenbasis:

{𝒖^1,𝒖^2,𝒏^},\{\hat{\bm{u}}_{1},\ \hat{\bm{u}}_{2},\ \hat{\bm{n}}\}, (C.4)

where 𝒖^1\hat{\bm{u}}_{1} and 𝒖^2\hat{\bm{u}}_{2} span the FP and 𝒏^\hat{\bm{n}} is perpendicular to it. The unit vector perpendicular to the FP, 𝒏^\hat{\bm{n}}, can be related to the cartesian basis (𝒆^r,𝒆^s,𝒆^i\bm{\hat{e}}_{r},\bm{\hat{e}}_{s},\bm{\hat{e}}_{i}) :

𝒏^=−a​𝒆^r+𝒆^s−b​𝒆^ia2+b2+1.\hat{\bm{n}}=\frac{-a\bm{\hat{e}}_{r}+\bm{\hat{e}}_{s}\ -b\bm{\hat{e}}_{i}}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+1}}. (C.5)

In this basis, the intrinsic dispersions are (σ1,σ2,σ3)(\sigma_{1},\ \sigma_{2},\ \sigma_{3}), with σ3\sigma_{3} being the thickness of the FP. The covariance matrix is then

𝚺r​s​i=𝑽​(σ12000σ22000σ32)​𝑽𝖳,\bm{\Sigma}_{rsi}=\bm{V}\begin{pmatrix}\sigma_{1}^{2}&0&0\\ 0&\sigma_{2}^{2}&0\\ 0&0&\sigma_{3}^{2}\end{pmatrix}\bm{V}^{\mathsf{T}}\,, (C.6)

where 𝑽=(𝒖^1,𝒖^2,𝒏^)\bm{V}=(\hat{\bm{u}}_{1},\hat{\bm{u}}_{2},\hat{\bm{n}}). For each lens of our sample, the conditional distribution of (r,i)(r,i), given the true value ss, remains Gaussian:

P​(r,i∣s)=𝒩​(𝝁R​I|s,𝚺R​I|s),P(r,i\mid s)=\mathcal{N}(\bm{\mu}_{RI|s},\ \bm{\Sigma}_{RI|s})\,, (C.7)

with conditional mean

𝝁r​i|s=𝝁r​i+𝚺r​i,s​Σs​s−1​(s−s¯),\bm{\mu}_{ri|s}=\bm{\mu}_{ri}+\bm{\Sigma}_{ri,s}\,\Sigma_{ss}^{-1}\,(s-\bar{s})\,, (C.8)

and conditional covariance

𝚺R​I|s=𝚺R​I−𝚺R​I,s​Σs​s−1​𝚺s,R​I,\bm{\Sigma}_{RI|s}=\bm{\Sigma}_{RI}-\bm{\Sigma}_{RI,s}\,\Sigma_{ss}^{-1}\bm{\Sigma}_{s,RI}, (C.9)

where 𝚺R​I\bm{\Sigma}_{RI} is the 2×22\times 2 covariance of (r,i)(r,i), 𝚺R​I,s\bm{\Sigma}_{RI,s} is the covariance between (r,i)(r,i) and ss, and Σs​s\Sigma_{ss} is the variance of ss

We emulate the measurement errors (δ​r,δ​i)(\delta r,\delta i) on the observable (r,i)(r,i) for each lens system from a multivariate Gaussian distribution, following [Said2020]:

(δ​rδ​i)\displaystyle\begin{pmatrix}\delta r\\[2.0pt] \delta i\end{pmatrix} ∼𝒩​(0,𝑬n),𝑬n=(ϵr2ρr​i​ϵr​ϵiρr​i​ϵr​ϵiϵi2),\displaystyle\sim\mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\bm{E}_{n}\;\right),\qquad\bm{E}_{n}=\begin{pmatrix}\epsilon_{r}^{2}&\rho_{ri}\,\epsilon_{r}\,\epsilon_{i}\\ \rho_{ri}\,\epsilon_{r}\,\epsilon_{i}&\epsilon_{i}^{2}\end{pmatrix}, (C.10)

where ρr​i=−0.95\rho_{ri}=-0.95 is the correlation between the photometric quantities rr and ii. ϵr\epsilon_{r} and ϵi\epsilon_{i} were found to be 0.049 dex (11%) and 0.073 dex (17%) in [Magoulas2012], from ground-based imaging. Although these could be substantially improved with Euclid [EuclidBretonniere2023], we adopt a conservative value of ϵr=0.041\epsilon_{r}=0.041 dex (10%) and ϵi=0.06\epsilon_{i}=0.06 dex (15%).

The observed quantities are

robs\displaystyle r_{\rm obs} =rtrue+δ​r,\displaystyle=r_{\rm true}+\delta r, iobs\displaystyle i_{\rm obs} =itrue+δ​i.\displaystyle=i_{\rm true}+\delta i. (C.11)

Adopting the same tri-variate Gaussian model and defining the total covariance matrix 𝑪n=𝚺+𝑬n\bm{C}_{n}=\bm{\Sigma}+\bm{E}_{n}. We can obtain an estimate of ss given the observed (robs,iobsr_{\rm obs},i_{\rm obs})

P​(s∣r,i)=𝒩​(μs|R​I,σs|R​I2),P(s\mid r,i)=\mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu_{s|RI},\,\sigma^{2}_{s|RI}\right), (C.12)

with conditional mean

μs|R​I=μs+𝑪s,R​I​𝑪R​I−1​((ri)−𝝁R​I)\mu_{s|RI}=\mu_{s}+\bm{C}_{s,RI}\,\bm{C}_{RI}^{-1}\left(\begin{pmatrix}r\\ i\end{pmatrix}-\bm{\mu}_{RI}\right) (C.13)

and conditional variance

σs|R​I2=Cs​s−𝑪s,R​I​𝑪R​I−1​𝑪R​I,s,\sigma^{2}_{s|RI}=C_{ss}-\bm{C}_{s,RI}\,\bm{C}_{RI}^{-1}\bm{C}_{RI,s}, (C.14)

where we partitioned the mean vector and covariance matrix as follows

𝝁=(𝝁R​Iμs),𝑪n=(𝑪R​I𝑪R​I,s𝑪s,R​ICs​s),𝝁R​I=(r¯,i¯).\bm{\mu}=\begin{pmatrix}\bm{\mu}_{RI}\\ \mu_{s}\end{pmatrix},\qquad\bm{C}_{n}=\begin{pmatrix}\bm{C}_{RI}&\bm{C}_{RI,s}\\ \bm{C}_{s,RI}&C_{ss}\end{pmatrix},\qquad\bm{\mu}_{RI}=(\bar{r},\bar{i}).\\ (C.15)

Appendix D Observer velocity effect on the Fundamental Plane observables

Here we describe how to use the FP relation (Eq. (4.13)) to estimate the velocity dispersion σFP\sigma_{\rm FP}, and in turn infer the boosted Einstein angle under the SIS assumption with σv=σFP\sigma_{v}=\sigma_{\rm FP}. To this end, it is necessary to understand how the effective radius ReR_{e} and the mean surface brightness IeI_{e} transform under a Lorentz boost. The radius can be estimated from the subtended angle and the lens redshift via a cosmological model. The subtended angle is subject to aberration, and the angular diameter distance dAd_{\rm A} is estimated from an observed redshift, which is Doppler shifted by 𝒗o\bm{v}_{o}. The subtended angle α\alpha is affected on average as the Einstein angle (Eq. (2.8))

α=α′​(1+vo2​c​cos⁡(θcm′)).\displaystyle\alpha=\alpha^{\prime}\left(1+\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos(\theta_{\rm cm}^{\prime})\right)\,. (D.1)

The radius can be expressed as

Re=α​dA=α′​d​[zl′]​(1+vo2​c​cos⁡(θcm′)+DLd​[zl′]​voc).\displaystyle R_{e}=\alpha d_{\rm A}=\alpha^{\prime}d[z_{l}^{\prime}]\left(1+\frac{v_{o}}{2c}\cos(\theta_{\rm cm}^{\prime})+\frac{D_{L}}{d[z_{l}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\,. (D.2)

In the following, we show that the mean surface brightness is invariant under a boost. It is defined as (Eq. (1.20) p.23 of [Binney:2008])

Ie≡L2​π​Re2,\displaystyle I_{e}\equiv\frac{L}{2\pi R_{e}^{2}}\,, (D.3)

and is often quoted in [W pc-2]. The luminosity LL [W] can be computed from the flux L=4​π​dL2​FL=4\pi d_{\rm L}^{2}F using the luminosity distance dLd_{\rm L} [m] and the flux FF [W m-2], which can itself be computed from the specific intensity I​(ν,𝒏^)I(\nu,\bm{\hat{n}}) [W m-2 Hz-1 sr-1] by integrating over frequency and solid angle over the relevant surface 𝒮\mathcal{S}

F=∫ℝ+dν​d2​∫𝒮𝒏^​I​(ν,𝒏^)​R​(ν).\displaystyle F=\int_{\mathbb{R}_{+}}\mathrm{d}\nu\mathrm{d}^{2}\int_{\mathcal{S}}\bm{\hat{n}}\,I(\nu,\bm{\hat{n}})R(\nu)\,. (D.4)

where R​(ν)R(\nu) is a filter that can be taken to be a tophat. Combining these steps, one obtains

Ie=L2​π​Re2=4​π​dL2​F2​dA2​Ω=2​π​dL2dA2​Ω​∫ℝ+dν​∫𝒮d2​𝒏^​I​(ν,𝒏^)​R​(ν)\displaystyle I_{e}=\frac{L}{2\pi R_{e}^{2}}=\frac{4\pi d_{\rm L}^{2}F}{2d_{\rm A}^{2}\Omega}=\frac{2\pi d_{\rm L}^{2}}{d_{\rm A}^{2}\Omega}\int_{\mathbb{R}_{+}}\mathrm{d}\nu\int_{\mathcal{S}}\mathrm{d}^{2}\bm{\hat{n}}\,I(\nu,\bm{\hat{n}})R(\nu) (D.5)

where Ω=π​α2\Omega=\pi\alpha^{2}. We assume for simplicity that Iν​(ν,𝒏^)I_{\nu}(\nu,\bm{\hat{n}}) is constant across the relevant angles spanned by 𝒏^∈𝒮\hat{\bm{n}}\in\mathcal{S}, i.e., I​(ν,𝒏^)=I​(ν)I(\nu,\bm{\hat{n}})=I(\nu). This leads to

Ie=2​π​dL2dA2​∫ℝ+dν​I​(ν)​R​(ν).\displaystyle I_{e}=\frac{2\pi d_{\rm L}^{2}}{d_{\rm A}^{2}}\int_{\mathbb{R}_{+}}\mathrm{d}\nu\,I(\nu)R(\nu)\,. (D.6)

Using Etherington’s reciprocity law dL=(1+zl)2​dAd_{\rm L}=(1+z_{l})^{2}d_{\rm A} [Etherington:1933asu], we obtain the simple expression

Ie=2​π​(1+zl)4​∫ℝ+dν​I​(ν)​R​(ν).\displaystyle I_{e}=2\pi(1+z_{l})^{4}\int_{\mathbb{R}_{+}}\mathrm{d}\nu I(\nu)R(\nu)\,. (D.7)

One can use the following transformation properties

I​(ν,𝒏^)\displaystyle I(\nu,\bm{\hat{n}}) =I′​(ν′,𝒏^′)​(νν′)3,\displaystyle=I^{\prime}(\nu^{\prime},\bm{\hat{n}^{\prime}})\left(\frac{\nu}{\nu^{\prime}}\right)^{3}\,, (D.8)
(1+zl)\displaystyle(1+z_{l}) =(1+zl′)​(1+ZL​voc),\displaystyle=(1+z_{l}^{\prime})\left(1+Z_{L}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\,, (D.9)
ν\displaystyle\nu =ννs​νsν′​ν′=(1+zl′)(1+zl)​ν′=ν′​(1−ZL​voc),\displaystyle=\frac{\nu}{\nu_{s}}\frac{\nu_{s}}{\nu^{\prime}}\nu^{\prime}=\frac{(1+z_{l}^{\prime})}{(1+z_{l})}\nu^{\prime}=\nu^{\prime}\left(1-Z_{L}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\,, (D.10)

where ZLZ_{L} was defined in Eq. (4.5) in terms of the observer’s lens and source peculiar velocities, and the identity for the specific intensity follows from the fact that I/ν3I/\nu^{3} is a Lorentz invariant. Combining these, the mean surface brightness transforms as

Ie\displaystyle I_{e} =2​π​(1+zl)4​∫R+dν​I​(ν)​R​(ν)\displaystyle=2\pi(1+z_{l})^{4}\int_{R_{+}}\mathrm{d}\nu I(\nu)R(\nu) (D.11)
=2​(1+zl′)4​(1+4​ZL​voc)​∫R+dν′​(1−ZL​voc)​I′​(ν′)​(νν′)3​R​(ν′)\displaystyle=2(1+z^{\prime}_{l})^{4}\left(1+4Z_{L}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\int_{R_{+}}\mathrm{d}\nu^{\prime}\left(1-Z_{L}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)I^{\prime}(\nu^{\prime})\left(\frac{\nu}{\nu^{\prime}}\right)^{3}R(\nu^{\prime})
=2​π​(1+zl′)4​(1+4​ZL​voc)​(1−4​ZL​voc)​∫R+dν′​I′​(ν′)​R​(ν′)\displaystyle=2\pi(1+z^{\prime}_{l})^{4}\left(1+4Z_{L}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\left(1-4Z_{L}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\int_{R_{+}}\mathrm{d}\nu^{\prime}I^{\prime}(\nu^{\prime})R(\nu^{\prime})
=2​π​(1+zl′)4​∫R+dν′​I′​(ν′)​R​(ν′)\displaystyle=2\pi(1+z_{l}^{\prime})^{4}\int_{R_{+}}\mathrm{d}\nu^{\prime}I^{\prime}(\nu^{\prime})R(\nu^{\prime})
=Ie′.\displaystyle=I_{e}^{\prime}\,. (D.12)

Hence, the mean surface brightness is invariant under a boost. Hence, σv\sigma_{v} is affected by the boost only via ReR_{e}. We obtain

a​log⁡σv\displaystyle a\log\sigma_{v} =log⁡Re−b​log⁡Ie−c\displaystyle=\log R_{e}-b\log I_{e}-c
=log⁡(α′​d​[zl′])−b​log⁡(Ie′)−c+log⁡(1+12​𝒗o⋅𝒏^′+DLd​[zl′]​voc).\displaystyle=\log(\alpha^{\prime}d[z_{l}^{\prime}])-b\log(I_{e}^{\prime})-c+\log\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\bm{v}_{o}\cdot\bm{\hat{n}^{\prime}}+\frac{D_{L}}{d[z_{l}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\,. (D.13)

Explicitly, the velocity dispersion σv\sigma_{v} can be expressed as

σv=σv′​(1+Σv​voc),\displaystyle\sigma_{v}=\sigma_{v}^{\prime}\left(1+\Sigma_{v}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\,, (D.14)

with the naive σv′\sigma_{v}^{\prime}, which would be extracted from observables if we didn’t account for the peculiar velocities and the correction Σv\Sigma_{v} defined as

σv′\displaystyle\sigma_{v}^{\prime} =(10−c​α′​d​[zl′]​(Ie′)−b)1/a,\displaystyle=\left(10^{-c}\alpha^{\prime}d[z_{l}^{\prime}](I_{e}^{\prime})^{-b}\right)^{1/a}\,, (D.15)
Σv\displaystyle\Sigma_{v} =(12​a​cos⁡(θcm′)+DLa​d​[zl′]).\displaystyle=\left(\frac{1}{2a}\cos(\theta_{\rm cm}^{\prime})+\frac{D_{L}}{ad[z_{l}^{\prime}]}\right)\,. (D.16)

We can further compute the effect on the Einstein angle if one assumes that σv=σFP\sigma_{v}=\sigma_{\rm FP} and adopts the SIS assumption

θE,FP′=4​π​σv2c2​dl​sds=4​π​σv′⁣2c2​d​[zl′,zs′]d​[zs′]​(1+DL​Sd​[zl′,zs′]​voc−DSd​[zs′]​voc+2​Σv​voc)\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E,\rm FP}=\frac{4\pi\sigma_{v}^{2}}{c^{2}}\frac{d_{ls}}{d_{s}}=\frac{4\pi\sigma_{v}^{\prime 2}}{c^{2}}\frac{d[z_{l}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime}]}{d[z_{s}^{\prime}]}\left(1+\frac{D_{LS}}{d[z_{l}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}-\frac{D_{S}}{d[z_{s}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}+2\Sigma_{v}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right) (D.17)

Replacing this expression for the Einstein angle in Eq. (2.8), we get

θE,FP′=4​π​σv′⁣2c2​d​[zl′,zs′]d​[zs′]​(1−12​voc​cos⁡(θcm′)+DL​Sd​[zl′,zs′]​voc−DSd​[zs′]​voc+2​Σv​voc).\displaystyle\theta^{\prime}_{E,\rm FP}=\frac{4\pi\sigma_{v}^{\prime 2}}{c^{2}}\frac{d[z_{l}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime}]}{d[z_{s}^{\prime}]}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\cos(\theta^{\prime}_{\rm cm})+\frac{D_{LS}}{d[z_{l}^{\prime},z_{s}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}-\frac{D_{S}}{d[z_{s}^{\prime}]}\frac{v_{o}}{c}+2\Sigma_{v}\frac{v_{o}}{c}\right)\,. (D.18)

This expression can be used to infer vov_{o} from the observables zl′z_{l}^{\prime}, zs′z_{s}^{\prime}, α′\alpha^{\prime}, Ie′I_{e}^{\prime}, θcm′\theta_{\rm cm}^{\prime}, and the FP parameters a,b,ca,b,c, which we assume are not affected by the boost. This is the expression that appears in (4.15).

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.
